					<div id="mw-content-text" class="mw-body-content"><div class="mw-subjectpageheader">
</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Class of animals with milk-producing glands</div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1320445320">.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}</style><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Mammal_(disambiguation)" class="mw-disambig" title="Mammal (disambiguation)">Mammal (disambiguation)</a>.</div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Several terms  redirect here. For other uses, see <a href="/wiki/Mammalian_(film)" title="Mammalian (film)"><i>Mammalian</i> (film)</a>&#32;and&#32;<a href="/wiki/Mammalia_(journal)" title="Mammalia (journal)"><i>Mammalia</i> (journal)</a>.</div>

<td colspan="2" style="text-align: center"><figure class="noresize mw-ext-imagemap-desc-bottom-right" typeof="mw:File"><span><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Mammal_collage.png/330px-Mammal_collage.png" decoding="async" width="300" height="375" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Mammal_collage.png/960px-Mammal_collage.png 2x" data-file-width="1200" data-file-height="1500" usemap="#ImageMap_59c2254333b323a7" resource="/wiki/File:Mammal_collage.png" /></span><map name="ImageMap_59c2254333b323a7"><area href="/wiki/Monotreme" coords="0,0,100,75" alt="Monotreme" title="Monotreme" /><area href="/wiki/Opossum" coords="100,0,200,75" alt="Opossum" title="Opossum" /><area href="/wiki/Kangaroo" coords="200,0,300,75" alt="Kangaroo" title="Kangaroo" /><area href="/wiki/Proboscidea" coords="0,75,100,150" alt="Proboscidea" title="Proboscidea" /><area href="/wiki/Armadillo" coords="100,75,200,150" alt="Armadillo" title="Armadillo" /><area href="/wiki/Sloth" coords="200,75,300,150" alt="Sloth" title="Sloth" /><area href="/wiki/Bat" coords="0,150,100,225" alt="Bat" title="Bat" /><area href="/wiki/Cetacea" coords="100,150,200,225" alt="Cetacea" title="Cetacea" /><area href="/wiki/Deer" coords="200,150,300,225" alt="Deer" title="Deer" /><area href="/wiki/Rhinoceros" coords="0,225,100,300" alt="Rhinoceros" title="Rhinoceros" /><area href="/wiki/Hedgehog" coords="100,225,200,300" alt="Hedgehog" title="Hedgehog" /><area href="/wiki/Pinniped" coords="200,225,300,300" alt="Pinniped" title="Pinniped" /><area href="/wiki/Raccoon" coords="0,300,100,375" alt="Raccoon" title="Raccoon" /><area href="/wiki/Rodent" coords="100,300,200,375" alt="Rodent" title="Rodent" /><area href="/wiki/Primate" coords="200,300,300,375" alt="Primate" title="Primate" /></map><figcaption></figcaption></figure>
</td></tr>

</tbody></table><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1289143401">@media(max-width:640px){body:not(.skin-minerva) .mw-parser-output .infobox{width:100%!important}body:not(.skin-minerva) .mw-parser-output .infobox th{width:50%}}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota tr{background:transparent!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota img{background:transparent}}@media screen and (prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota tr{background:transparent!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox.biota img{background:white}}.mw-parser-output .infobox.biota .taxobox-edit-taxonomy img{background:transparent!important}body.skin-vector .mw-parser-output table.biota.infobox{margin-top:0.5em}body.skin--responsive .mw-parser-output table.biota.infobox tr.taxonrow2 td{padding:2px 10px}</style>
<p>A <b>mammal</b> (from <a href="/wiki/Latin_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Latin language">Latin</a> <i> <i lang="la"><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mamma#Latin" class="extiw" title="wikt:mamma">mamma</a></i></i> <span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">breast</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-1"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>1<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> is a <a href="/wiki/Vertebrate" title="Vertebrate">vertebrate</a> animal of the <a href="/wiki/Class_(biology)" title="Class (biology)">class</a> <b>Mammalia</b> (<span class="rt-commentedText nowrap"><span class="IPA nopopups noexcerpt" lang="en-fonipa"><a href="/wiki/Help:IPA/English" title="Help:IPA/English">/<span style="border-bottom:1px dotted"><span title="&#39;m&#39; in &#39;my&#39;">m</span><span title="/ə/: &#39;a&#39; in &#39;about&#39;">ə</span><span title="/ˈ/: primary stress follows">ˈ</span><span title="&#39;m&#39; in &#39;my&#39;">m</span><span title="/eɪ/: &#39;a&#39; in &#39;face&#39;">eɪ</span><span title="&#39;l&#39; in &#39;lie&#39;">l</span><span title="/i/: &#39;y&#39; in &#39;happy&#39;">i</span><span title="/./: syllable break">.</span><span title="/ə/: &#39;a&#39; in &#39;about&#39;">ə</span></span>/</a></span></span>). Mammals are characterised by the presence of <a href="/wiki/Milk" title="Milk">milk</a>-producing <a href="/wiki/Mammary_gland" title="Mammary gland">mammary glands</a> for feeding their young, a broad <a href="/wiki/Neocortex" title="Neocortex">neocortex</a> region of the brain, <a href="/wiki/Fur" title="Fur">fur</a> or <a href="/wiki/Hair" title="Hair">hair</a>, and three <a href="/wiki/Evolution_of_mammalian_auditory_ossicles" title="Evolution of mammalian auditory ossicles">middle ear bones</a>. These characteristics distinguish them from <a href="/wiki/Reptile" title="Reptile">reptiles</a> and <a href="/wiki/Bird" title="Bird">birds</a>, from which their ancestors <a href="/wiki/Genetic_divergence" title="Genetic divergence">diverged</a> in the <a href="/wiki/Carboniferous" title="Carboniferous">Carboniferous</a> period over 300&#160;million years ago. Around 6,640 <a href="/wiki/Neontology#Extant_taxon" title="Neontology">extant</a> species of mammals have been described and divided into 27 <a href="/wiki/Order_(biology)" title="Order (biology)">orders</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The study of mammals is called <a href="/wiki/Mammalogy" title="Mammalogy">mammalogy</a>.
</p><p>The largest orders of mammals, by number of <a href="/wiki/Species" title="Species">species</a>, are the <a href="/wiki/Rodent" title="Rodent">rodents</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bat" title="Bat">bats</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Eulipotyphla" title="Eulipotyphla">eulipotyphlans</a> (including <a href="/wiki/Hedgehog" title="Hedgehog">hedgehogs</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mole_(animal)" title="Mole (animal)">moles</a> and <a href="/wiki/Shrew" title="Shrew">shrews</a>). The next three are the <a href="/wiki/Primate" title="Primate">primates</a> (including <a href="/wiki/Human" title="Human">humans</a>, <a href="/wiki/Monkey" title="Monkey">monkeys</a> and <a href="/wiki/Lemur" title="Lemur">lemurs</a>), the <a href="/wiki/Artiodactyl" class="mw-redirect" title="Artiodactyl">even-toed ungulates</a> (including <a href="/wiki/Suidae" title="Suidae">pigs</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bovid" class="mw-redirect" title="Bovid">bovids</a> and <a href="/wiki/Cetacean" title="Cetacean">whales</a>), and the <a href="/wiki/Carnivora" title="Carnivora">Carnivora</a> (including <a href="/wiki/Felidae" title="Felidae">cats</a>, <a href="/wiki/Canidae" title="Canidae">dogs</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Pinniped" title="Pinniped">seals</a>).
</p><p>Mammals are the only living members of <a href="/wiki/Synapsida" title="Synapsida">Synapsida</a>; this <a href="/wiki/Clade" title="Clade">clade</a>, together with <a href="/wiki/Sauropsida" title="Sauropsida">Sauropsida</a> (reptiles and birds), constitutes the larger <a href="/wiki/Amniote" title="Amniote">Amniota</a> clade. Early synapsids are referred to as "<a href="/wiki/Pelycosaur" title="Pelycosaur">pelycosaurs</a>." The more advanced <a href="/wiki/Therapsida" title="Therapsida">therapsids</a> became dominant during the <a href="/wiki/Guadalupian" title="Guadalupian">Guadalupian</a>. Mammals originated from <a href="/wiki/Cynodontia" title="Cynodontia">cynodonts</a>, an advanced group of therapsids, during the Late <a href="/wiki/Triassic" title="Triassic">Triassic</a> to Early <a href="/wiki/Jurassic" title="Jurassic">Jurassic</a>. Mammals achieved their modern diversity in the <a href="/wiki/Paleogene" title="Paleogene">Paleogene</a> and <a href="/wiki/Neogene" title="Neogene">Neogene</a> periods of the <a href="/wiki/Cenozoic" title="Cenozoic">Cenozoic</a> era, after the <a href="/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event" title="Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event">extinction of non-avian dinosaurs</a>, and have been the <a href="/wiki/Dominance_(ecology)" title="Dominance (ecology)">dominant</a> terrestrial animal group from 66&#160;million years ago to the present.
</p><p>The basic mammalian body type is <a href="/wiki/Quadrupedalism" title="Quadrupedalism">quadrupedal</a>, with most mammals using four <a href="/wiki/Limb_(anatomy)" title="Limb (anatomy)">limbs</a> for <a href="/wiki/Terrestrial_locomotion" title="Terrestrial locomotion">terrestrial locomotion</a>; but in some, the limbs are adapted for life <a href="/wiki/Marine_mammal" title="Marine mammal">at sea</a>, <a href="/wiki/Flying_and_gliding_animals" title="Flying and gliding animals">in the air</a>, <a href="/wiki/Arboreal_locomotion" title="Arboreal locomotion">in trees</a> or <a href="/wiki/Fossorial" title="Fossorial">underground</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Bipedalism" title="Bipedalism">bipeds</a> have adapted to move using only the two lower limbs, while the rear limbs of <a href="/wiki/Cetacea" class="mw-redirect" title="Cetacea">cetaceans</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Sirenia" title="Sirenia">sea cows</a> are mere internal <a href="/wiki/Vestigiality" title="Vestigiality">vestiges</a>. Mammals range in size from the 30–40 millimetres (1.2–1.6&#160;in) <a href="/wiki/Bumblebee_bat" class="mw-redirect" title="Bumblebee bat">bumblebee bat</a> to the 30 metres (98&#160;ft) <a href="/wiki/Blue_whale" title="Blue whale">blue whale</a>—possibly the largest animal to have ever lived. Maximum lifespan varies from two years for the shrew to 211 years for the <a href="/wiki/Bowhead_whale" title="Bowhead whale">bowhead whale</a>. All modern mammals give birth to live young, except the five species of <a href="/wiki/Monotreme" title="Monotreme">monotremes</a>, which lay eggs. The most species-rich group is the <a href="/wiki/Viviparity" title="Viviparity">viviparous</a> <a href="/wiki/Placentalia" title="Placentalia">placental mammals</a>, so named for the temporary organ (<a href="/wiki/Placenta" title="Placenta">placenta</a>) used by offspring to draw nutrition from the mother during <a href="/wiki/Gestation" title="Gestation">gestation</a>.
</p><p>Most mammals are <a href="/wiki/Animal_cognition" title="Animal cognition">intelligent</a>, with some possessing large brains, <a href="/wiki/Self-awareness" title="Self-awareness">self-awareness</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Tool_use_by_non-humans" title="Tool use by non-humans">tool use</a>. Mammals can communicate and vocalise in several ways, including the production of <a href="/wiki/Ultrasound" title="Ultrasound">ultrasound</a>, <a href="/wiki/Territory_(animal)#Scent_marking" title="Territory (animal)">scent marking</a>, <a href="/wiki/Alarm_signal" title="Alarm signal">alarm signals</a>, <a href="/wiki/Animal_song" title="Animal song">singing</a>, <a href="/wiki/Animal_echolocation" title="Animal echolocation">echolocation</a>; and, in the case of humans, complex <a href="/wiki/Language" title="Language">language</a>. Mammals can organise themselves into <a href="/wiki/Fission%E2%80%93fusion_society" title="Fission–fusion society">fission–fusion societies</a>, <a href="/wiki/Harem_(zoology)" title="Harem (zoology)">harems</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Hierarchy" title="Hierarchy">hierarchies</a>—but can also be solitary and <a href="/wiki/Territory_(animal)" title="Territory (animal)">territorial</a>. Most mammals are <a href="/wiki/Polygyny_in_animals" title="Polygyny in animals">polygynous</a>, but some can be <a href="/wiki/Monogamy_in_animals" title="Monogamy in animals">monogamous</a> or <a href="/wiki/Polyandry_in_animals" title="Polyandry in animals">polyandrous</a>.
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Domestication" title="Domestication">Domestication</a> of many types of mammals by humans played a major role in the <a href="/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution" title="Neolithic Revolution">Neolithic Revolution</a>, and resulted in <a href="/wiki/Agriculture" title="Agriculture">farming</a> replacing <a href="/wiki/Hunter-gatherer" title="Hunter-gatherer">hunting and gathering</a> as the primary source of food for humans. This led to a major restructuring of human societies from nomadic to sedentary, with more co-operation among larger and larger groups, and ultimately the development of the first <a href="/wiki/Civilisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Civilisation">civilisations</a>. Domesticated mammals provided, and continue to provide, power for transport and agriculture, as well as food (<a href="/wiki/Meat" title="Meat">meat</a> and <a href="/wiki/Dairy_product" title="Dairy product">dairy products</a>), <a href="/wiki/Fur" title="Fur">fur</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Leather" title="Leather">leather</a>. Mammals are also <a href="/wiki/Hunting" title="Hunting">hunted</a> and raced for sport, kept as <a href="/wiki/Pet" title="Pet">pets</a> and <a href="/wiki/Working_animal" title="Working animal">working animals</a> of various types, and are used as <a href="/wiki/Model_organism" title="Model organism">model organisms</a> in science. Mammals have been depicted in <a href="/wiki/Art" title="Art">art</a> since <a href="/wiki/Paleolithic" title="Paleolithic">Paleolithic</a> times, and appear in literature, film, mythology, and religion. The decline in numbers and <a href="/wiki/Extinction" title="Extinction">extinction</a> of many mammals is primarily driven by human <a href="/wiki/Poaching" title="Poaching">poaching</a> and <a href="/wiki/Habitat_destruction" title="Habitat destruction">habitat destruction</a>, primarily <a href="/wiki/Deforestation" title="Deforestation">deforestation</a>.
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r886046785">.mw-parser-output .toclimit-2 .toclevel-1 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-3 .toclevel-2 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-4 .toclevel-3 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-5 .toclevel-4 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-6 .toclevel-5 ul,.mw-parser-output .toclimit-7 .toclevel-6 ul{display:none}</style><div class="toclimit-4"><meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" /></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Classification">Classification</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Mammal_classification" title="Mammal classification">Mammal classification</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/List_of_mammals" title="List of mammals">List of mammals</a></div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1290014865">.mw-parser-output <div class="pie-thumb thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner"><div class="smooth-pie-container" style="">
<div class="smooth-pie-legend-container"><div class="smooth-pie-caption">Over 70% of mammal species are in the orders <a href="/wiki/Rodent" title="Rodent">Rodentia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Chiroptera" class="mw-redirect" title="Chiroptera">Chiroptera</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Eulipotyphla" title="Eulipotyphla">Eulipotyphla</a>.</div>
<ol class="smooth-pie-legend">
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#63aafe;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Rodentia" class="mw-redirect" title="Rodentia">Rodentia</a> (40.5%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#dd2d32;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Chiroptera" class="mw-redirect" title="Chiroptera">Chiroptera</a> (22.2%)</span></li>

<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#6711ff;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Carnivora" title="Carnivora">Carnivora</a> (4.70%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#865357;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Diprotodontia" title="Diprotodontia">Diprotodontia</a> (2.30%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#00ccff;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Didelphimorphia" class="mw-redirect" title="Didelphimorphia">Didelphimorphia</a> (1.90%)</span></li>

<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#ccffcc;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Dasyuromorphia" title="Dasyuromorphia">Dasyuromorphia</a> (1.30%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#ffff99;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Afrosoricida" title="Afrosoricida">Afrosoricida</a> (0.80%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#ff99cc;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Armadillo" title="Armadillo">Cingulata</a> (0.30%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#33cccc;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Macroscelidea" class="mw-redirect" title="Macroscelidea">Macroscelidea</a> (0.30%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#cc99ff;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Peramelemorphia" title="Peramelemorphia">Peramelemorphia</a> (0.30%)</span></li>

<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#e7d4e8;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Shrew_opossum" title="Shrew opossum">Paucituberculata</a> (0.10%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#d73027;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Pholidota" class="mw-redirect" title="Pholidota">Pholidota</a> (0.10%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#99ccff;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Hyracoidea" class="mw-redirect" title="Hyracoidea">Hyracoidea</a> (0.09%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#ff6600;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Monotremata" class="mw-redirect" title="Monotremata">Monotremata</a> (0.08%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#4575b4;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Sirenia" title="Sirenia">Sirenia</a> (0.06%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#003366;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Elephant" title="Elephant">Proboscidea</a> (0.05%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#ccffff;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Dermoptera" class="mw-redirect" title="Dermoptera">Dermoptera</a> (0.03%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#1b7837;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Monito_del_monte" title="Monito del monte">Microbiotheria</a> (0.03%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#7fbf7b;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Marsupial_mole" title="Marsupial mole">Notoryctemorphia</a> (0.03%)</span></li>
<li><span class="l-color" style="-webkit-print-color-adjust: exact; print-color-adjust: exact;background:#d9f0d3;color:#000"></span><span class="l-label"><a href="/wiki/Aardvark" title="Aardvark">Tubulidentata</a> (0.02%)</span></li>
</ol>
</div>

<div class="smooth-pie-border"></div></div>
</div></div></div>
<p>Mammal classification has been through several revisions since <a href="/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus" title="Carl Linnaeus">Carl Linnaeus</a> initially defined the class, and at present<sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items" title="Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers"><span title="The time period mentioned near this tag is ambiguous. (August 2024)">when?</span></a></i>&#93;</sup>, no classification system is universally accepted. McKenna &amp; Bell (1997) and Wilson &amp; Reeder (2005) provide useful recent compendiums.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/George_Gaylord_Simpson" title="George Gaylord Simpson">Simpson</a> (1945)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Simpson-1945&#95;4-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Simpson-1945-4"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>4<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> provides <a href="/wiki/Systematics" title="Systematics">systematics</a> of mammal origins and relationships that had been taught universally until the end of the 20th&#160;century.
However, since 1945, a large amount of new and more detailed information has gradually been found: The <a href="/wiki/Fossil_record" class="mw-redirect" title="Fossil record">paleontological record</a> has been recalibrated, and the intervening years have seen much debate and progress concerning the theoretical underpinnings of systematisation itself, partly through the new concept of <a href="/wiki/Cladistics" title="Cladistics">cladistics</a>. Though fieldwork and lab work progressively outdated Simpson's classification, it remains the closest thing to an official classification of mammals, despite its known issues.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Szalay&#95;5-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Szalay-5"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Most mammals, including the six most species-rich orders, belong to the placental group. The three largest orders in numbers of species are <a href="/wiki/Rodent" title="Rodent">Rodentia</a>: <a href="/wiki/Mouse" title="Mouse">mice</a>, <a href="/wiki/Rat" title="Rat">rats</a>, <a href="/wiki/Porcupine" title="Porcupine">porcupines</a>, <a href="/wiki/Beaver" title="Beaver">beavers</a>, <a href="/wiki/Capybara" title="Capybara">capybaras</a>, and other gnawing mammals; <a href="/wiki/Chiroptera" class="mw-redirect" title="Chiroptera">Chiroptera</a>: bats; and <a href="/wiki/Eulipotyphla" title="Eulipotyphla">Eulipotyphla</a>: <a href="/wiki/Shrew" title="Shrew">shrews</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mole_(animal)" title="Mole (animal)">moles</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Solenodon" title="Solenodon">solenodons</a>. The next three biggest orders, depending on the <a href="/wiki/Biological_classification" class="mw-redirect" title="Biological classification">biological classification</a> scheme used, are the <a href="/wiki/Primate" title="Primate">primates</a>: <a href="/wiki/Ape" title="Ape">apes</a>, <a href="/wiki/Monkey" title="Monkey">monkeys</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Lemur" title="Lemur">lemurs</a>; <a href="/wiki/Cetartiodactyla" class="mw-redirect" title="Cetartiodactyla">Cetartiodactyla</a>: <a href="/wiki/Whale" title="Whale">whales</a> and <a href="/wiki/Even-toed_ungulate" class="mw-redirect" title="Even-toed ungulate">even-toed ungulates</a>; and <a href="/wiki/Carnivora" title="Carnivora">Carnivora</a> which includes <a href="/wiki/Cat" title="Cat">cats</a>, <a href="/wiki/Dog" title="Dog">dogs</a>, <a href="/wiki/Weasel" title="Weasel">weasels</a>, <a href="/wiki/Bear" title="Bear">bears</a>, <a href="/wiki/Pinniped" title="Pinniped">seals</a>, and allies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MSW3intro&#95;6-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MSW3intro-6"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to <i><a href="/wiki/Mammal_Species_of_the_World" title="Mammal Species of the World">Mammal Species of the World</a></i>, 5,416&#160;species were identified in 2006. These were grouped into 1,229&#160;<a href="/wiki/Genus" title="Genus">genera</a>, 153&#160;<a href="/wiki/Family_(biology)" title="Family (biology)">families</a> and 29&#160;orders.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MSW3intro&#95;6-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MSW3intro-6"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In 2008, the <a href="/wiki/International_Union_for_Conservation_of_Nature" title="International Union for Conservation of Nature">International Union for Conservation of Nature</a> (IUCN) completed a five-year Global Mammal Assessment for its <a href="/wiki/IUCN_Red_List" title="IUCN Red List">IUCN Red List</a>, which counted 5,488&#160;species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>7<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to research published in the <i><a href="/wiki/Journal_of_Mammalogy" title="Journal of Mammalogy">Journal of Mammalogy</a></i> in 2018, the number of recognised mammal species is 6,495, including 96&#160;recently extinct.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-8"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>8<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Definitions">Definitions <span class="anchor" id="variations"></span></h3></div>
<p>The word "<a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mammal" class="extiw" title="wikt:mammal">mammal</a>" is modern, from the scientific name <i>Mammalia</i> coined by <a href="/wiki/Carl_Linnaeus" title="Carl Linnaeus">Carl Linnaeus</a> in 1758, derived from the <a href="/wiki/Latin_language" class="mw-redirect" title="Latin language">Latin</a> <i><a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/mamma#Latin" class="extiw" title="wikt:mamma">mamma</a></i> ("teat, pap"). In an influential 1988 paper, Timothy Rowe defined Mammalia <a href="/wiki/Phylogenetically" class="mw-redirect" title="Phylogenetically">phylogenetically</a> as the <a href="/wiki/Crown_group" title="Crown group">crown group</a> of mammals, the <a href="/wiki/Clade" title="Clade">clade</a> consisting of the <a href="/wiki/Most_recent_common_ancestor" title="Most recent common ancestor">most recent common ancestor</a> of living <a href="/wiki/Monotreme" title="Monotreme">monotremes</a> (<a href="/wiki/Echidna" title="Echidna">echidnas</a> and <a href="/wiki/Platypus" title="Platypus">platypuses</a>) and <a href="/wiki/Theria" title="Theria">therians</a> (<a href="/wiki/Marsupial" title="Marsupial">marsupials</a> and <a href="/wiki/Placental" class="mw-redirect" title="Placental">placentals</a>) and all descendants of that ancestor.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-9" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-9"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>9<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since this ancestor lived in the <a href="/wiki/Jurassic" title="Jurassic">Jurassic</a> Period, Rowe's definition excludes all animals from the earlier <a href="/wiki/Triassic" title="Triassic">Triassic</a>, despite the fact that Triassic fossils in the <a href="/wiki/Haramiyida" title="Haramiyida">Haramiyida</a> have been referred to the Mammalia since the mid-19th century.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-10" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> If Mammalia is considered as the crown group, its origin can be roughly dated as the first known appearance of animals more closely related to some extant mammals than to others. <i><a href="/wiki/Ambondro_mahabo" title="Ambondro mahabo">Ambondro</a></i> is more closely related to monotremes than to therian mammals while <i><a href="/wiki/Amphilestes" title="Amphilestes">Amphilestes</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Amphitherium" title="Amphitherium">Amphitherium</a></i> are more closely related to the therians; as fossils of all three genera are dated about <span class="nourlexpansion plainlinks" style="white-space:nowrap;"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://geoltime.github.io/?Ma=167">167</a> million years ago</span> in the <a href="/wiki/Middle_Jurassic" title="Middle Jurassic">Middle Jurassic</a>, this is a reasonable estimate for the appearance of the crown group.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (October 2025)">citation needed</span></a></i>&#93;</sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/T._S._Kemp" title="T. S. Kemp">T. S. Kemp</a> has provided a more traditional definition: "<a href="/wiki/Synapsid" class="mw-redirect" title="Synapsid">Synapsids</a> that possess a <a href="/wiki/Dentary" class="mw-redirect" title="Dentary">dentary</a>–<a href="/wiki/Squamosal_bone" title="Squamosal bone">squamosal</a> jaw articulation and <a href="/wiki/Occlusion_(dentistry)" title="Occlusion (dentistry)">occlusion</a> between upper and lower molars with a transverse component to the movement" or, equivalently in Kemp's view, the clade originating with the last common ancestor of <i><a href="/wiki/Sinoconodon" title="Sinoconodon">Sinoconodon</a></i> and living mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-11" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-11"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>11<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The earliest-known synapsid satisfying Kemp's definitions is <i><a href="/wiki/Tikitherium" title="Tikitherium">Tikitherium</a></i>, dated <span class="nourlexpansion plainlinks" style="white-space:nowrap;"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://geoltime.github.io/?Ma=225">225</a> Ma</span>, so the appearance of mammals in this broader sense can be given this <a href="/wiki/Late_Triassic" title="Late Triassic">Late Triassic</a> date.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-12" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>12<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-13" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-13"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, this animal may have actually evolved during the Neogene.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;14-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-14"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Molecular_classification_of_placentals">Molecular classification of placentals</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:OrthoMaM_v10b_2019_116genera_circular_tree.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/OrthoMaM_v10b_2019_116genera_circular_tree.svg/500px-OrthoMaM_v10b_2019_116genera_circular_tree.svg.png" decoding="async" width="340" height="301" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/OrthoMaM_v10b_2019_116genera_circular_tree.svg/960px-OrthoMaM_v10b_2019_116genera_circular_tree.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="1674" data-file-height="1481" /></a><figcaption>Genus-level molecular phylogeny of 116 extant mammals inferred from the gene tree information of 14,509 <a href="/wiki/Coding_region" title="Coding region">coding DNA sequences</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Scornavacca2019&#95;15-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Scornavacca2019-15"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The major clades are coloured: marsupials (magenta), xenarthrans (orange), afrotherians (red), laurasiatherians (green), and euarchontoglirans (blue).</figcaption></figure>
<p>As of the early 21st century, molecular studies based on <a href="/wiki/DNA" title="DNA">DNA</a> analysis have suggested new relationships among mammal families. Most of these findings have been independently validated by <a href="/wiki/Retrotransposon" title="Retrotransposon">retrotransposon</a> <a href="/wiki/Retrotransposon_marker" title="Retrotransposon marker">presence/absence data</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kriegs2006&#95;16-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kriegs2006-16"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Classification systems based on molecular studies reveal three major groups or lineages of placentals—<a href="/wiki/Afrotheria" title="Afrotheria">Afrotheria</a>, <a href="/wiki/Xenarthra" title="Xenarthra">Xenarthra</a> and <a href="/wiki/Boreoeutheria" title="Boreoeutheria">Boreoeutheria</a>—which <a href="/wiki/Speciation" title="Speciation">diverged</a> in the <a href="/wiki/Cretaceous" title="Cretaceous">Cretaceous</a>. The relationships between these three lineages is contentious, and all three possible hypotheses have been proposed with respect to which group is <a href="/wiki/Basal_(phylogenetics)" title="Basal (phylogenetics)">basal</a>. These hypotheses are <a href="/wiki/Atlantogenata" title="Atlantogenata">Atlantogenata</a> (basal Boreoeutheria), <a href="/wiki/Epitheria" title="Epitheria">Epitheria</a> (basal Xenarthra) and <a href="/wiki/Exafroplacentalia" title="Exafroplacentalia">Exafroplacentalia</a> (basal Afrotheria).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nishiharaetal2009&#95;17-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nishiharaetal2009-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Boreoeutheria in turn contains two major lineages—<a href="/wiki/Euarchontoglires" title="Euarchontoglires">Euarchontoglires</a> and <a href="/wiki/Laurasiatheria" title="Laurasiatheria">Laurasiatheria</a>.
</p><p>Estimates for the divergence times between these three placental groups range from 105 to 120&#160;million years ago, depending on the type of DNA used (such as <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_DNA" title="Nuclear DNA">nuclear</a> or <a href="/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA" title="Mitochondrial DNA">mitochondrial</a>)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-18" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-18"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and varying interpretations of <a href="/wiki/Paleogeographic" class="mw-redirect" title="Paleogeographic">paleogeographic</a> data.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nishiharaetal2009&#95;17-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nishiharaetal2009-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Mammal phylogeny according to Álvarez-Carretero <i>et al.</i>, 2022:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Evolution">Evolution</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Evolution_of_mammals" title="Evolution of mammals">Evolution of mammals</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Origins">Origins</h3></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Synapsida" title="Synapsida">Synapsida</a>, a clade that contains mammals and their extinct relatives, originated during the <a href="/wiki/Pennsylvanian_(geology)" title="Pennsylvanian (geology)">Pennsylvanian subperiod</a> (~323&#160;million to ~300&#160;million years ago), when they split from the reptile lineage. Crown group mammals evolved from earlier <a href="/wiki/Mammaliaformes" title="Mammaliaformes">mammaliaforms</a> during the <a href="/wiki/Early_Jurassic" title="Early Jurassic">Early Jurassic</a>. The cladogram takes Mammalia to be the crown group.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Liaoconodon2011&#95;20-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Liaoconodon2011-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="clade"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1344960044" />
<table class="clade">


<tbody><tr>
<td class="clade-label first"><a href="/wiki/Mammaliaformes" title="Mammaliaformes">Mammaliaformes</a>
</td>
<td rowspan="2" class="clade-leaf">
<div><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1344960044" />
<table class="clade">


<tbody><tr>
<td class="clade-label first">

</p>
</td></tr>

<tr>
<td class="clade-label"><b>Mammalia</b>
</td>
<td rowspan="2" class="clade-leaf">

<td rowspan="2" class="clade-leaf">

</p>
</td></tr>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Evolution_from_older_amniotes">Evolution from older amniotes</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Skull_synapsida_1.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Skull_synapsida_1.png/250px-Skull_synapsida_1.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="114" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Skull_synapsida_1.png/500px-Skull_synapsida_1.png 2x" data-file-width="568" data-file-height="260" /></a><figcaption>The original synapsid skull structure contains one <a href="/wiki/Temporal_fenestrae" class="mw-redirect" title="Temporal fenestrae">temporal opening</a> behind the <a href="/wiki/Eye_socket" class="mw-redirect" title="Eye socket">orbitals</a>, in a fairly low position on the skull (lower right in this image). This opening might have assisted in containing the jaw muscles of these organisms which could have increased their biting strength.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The first fully terrestrial <a href="/wiki/Vertebrate" title="Vertebrate">vertebrates</a> were <a href="/wiki/Amniote" title="Amniote">amniotes</a>. Like their amphibious early <a href="/wiki/Tetrapod" title="Tetrapod">tetrapod</a> predecessors, they had lungs and limbs. Amniotic eggs, however, have internal membranes that allow the developing <a href="/wiki/Embryo" title="Embryo">embryo</a> to breathe but keep water in. Hence, amniotes can lay eggs on dry land, while <a href="/wiki/Amphibian" title="Amphibian">amphibians</a> generally need to lay their eggs in water.
</p><p>The first amniotes apparently arose in the Pennsylvanian subperiod of the <a href="/wiki/Carboniferous" title="Carboniferous">Carboniferous</a>. They descended from earlier <a href="/wiki/Reptiliomorpha" title="Reptiliomorpha">reptiliomorph</a> amphibious tetrapods,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-AhlbergMilner1994OriginOfTetrapods&#95;21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AhlbergMilner1994OriginOfTetrapods-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which lived on land that was already inhabited by <a href="/wiki/Insect" title="Insect">insects</a> and other invertebrates as well as <a href="/wiki/Fern" title="Fern">ferns</a>, <a href="/wiki/Moss" title="Moss">mosses</a> and other plants. Within a few million years, two important amniote lineages became distinct: the <a href="/wiki/Synapsid" class="mw-redirect" title="Synapsid">synapsids</a>, which would later include the common ancestor of the mammals; and the <a href="/wiki/Sauropsid" class="mw-redirect" title="Sauropsid">sauropsids</a>, which now include <a href="/wiki/Turtle" title="Turtle">turtles</a>, <a href="/wiki/Lizard" title="Lizard">lizards</a>, <a href="/wiki/Snake" title="Snake">snakes</a>, <a href="/wiki/Crocodilian" class="mw-redirect" title="Crocodilian">crocodilians</a> and <a href="/wiki/Dinosaur" title="Dinosaur">dinosaurs</a> (including <a href="/wiki/Bird" title="Bird">birds</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-22" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-22"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Synapsids have a single hole (<a href="/wiki/Temporal_fenestra" title="Temporal fenestra">temporal fenestra</a>) low on each side of the skull. Primitive synapsids included the largest and fiercest animals of the early <a href="/wiki/Permian" title="Permian">Permian</a> such as <i><a href="/wiki/Dimetrodon" title="Dimetrodon">Dimetrodon</a></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-23" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Nonmammalian synapsids were traditionally—and incorrectly—called "mammal-like reptiles" or <a href="/wiki/Pelycosaur" title="Pelycosaur">pelycosaurs</a>; we now know they were neither reptiles nor part of reptile lineage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kemp2006&#95;24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kemp2006-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bennett1986&#95;25-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bennett1986-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Therapsid" class="mw-redirect" title="Therapsid">Therapsids</a>, a group of synapsids, evolved in the <a href="/wiki/Guadalupian" title="Guadalupian">Middle Permian</a>, about 265&#160;million years ago, and became the dominant land vertebrates.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kemp2006&#95;24-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kemp2006-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They differ from basal <a href="/wiki/Eupelycosauria" title="Eupelycosauria">eupelycosaurs</a> in several features of the skull and jaws, including: larger skulls and <a href="/wiki/Incisor" title="Incisor">incisors</a> which are equal in size in therapsids, but not for eupelycosaurs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kemp2006&#95;24-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kemp2006-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The therapsid lineage leading to mammals went through a series of stages, beginning with animals that were very similar to their early synapsid ancestors and ending with <a href="/wiki/Probainognathia" title="Probainognathia">probainognathian</a> <a href="/wiki/Cynodont" class="mw-redirect" title="Cynodont">cynodonts</a>, some of which could easily be mistaken for mammals. Those stages were characterised by:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kermack1984&#95;26-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kermack1984-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul><li>The gradual development of a bony secondary <a href="/wiki/Hard_palate" title="Hard palate">palate</a>.</li>
<li>Abrupt acquisition of <a href="/wiki/Endothermy" class="mw-redirect" title="Endothermy">endothermy</a> among <a href="/wiki/Mammaliamorpha" title="Mammaliamorpha">Mammaliamorpha</a>, thus prior to the origin of mammals by 30–50 millions of years <i><b><sup id="cite&#95;ref-27" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></b></i>.</li>
<li>Progression towards an erect limb posture, which would increase the animals' stamina by avoiding <a href="/wiki/Carrier%27s_constraint" title="Carrier&#39;s constraint">Carrier's constraint</a>. But this process was slow and erratic: for example, all herbivorous nonmammaliaform therapsids retained sprawling limbs (some late forms may have had semierect hind limbs); Permian carnivorous therapsids had sprawling forelimbs, and some late Permian ones also had semisprawling hindlimbs. In fact, modern monotremes still have semisprawling limbs.</li>
<li>The <a href="/wiki/Mandible#Other_vertebrates" title="Mandible">dentary</a> gradually became the main bone of the lower jaw which, by the Triassic, progressed towards the fully mammalian jaw (the lower consisting only of the dentary) and middle ear (which is constructed by the bones that were previously used to construct the jaws of reptiles).</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="First_mammals">First mammals</h3></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Permian%E2%80%93Triassic_extinction_event" title="Permian–Triassic extinction event">Permian–Triassic extinction event</a> about 252&#160;million years ago, which was a prolonged event due to the accumulation of several extinction pulses, ended the dominance of carnivorous therapsids.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the early Triassic, most medium to large land carnivore niches were taken over by <a href="/wiki/Archosaur" title="Archosaur">archosaurs</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-29" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which, over an extended period (35&#160;million years), came to include the <a href="/wiki/Crocodylomorpha" title="Crocodylomorpha">crocodylomorphs</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-30" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the <a href="/wiki/Pterosaur" title="Pterosaur">pterosaurs</a> and the dinosaurs;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-31" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> however, large cynodonts like <i><a href="/wiki/Trucidocynodon" title="Trucidocynodon">Trucidocynodon</a></i> and <a href="/wiki/Traversodontidae" title="Traversodontidae">traversodontids</a> still occupied large sized carnivorous and herbivorous niches respectively. By the Jurassic, the dinosaurs had come to dominate the large terrestrial herbivore niches as well.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-32" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The first mammals (in Kemp's sense) appeared in the Late Triassic epoch (about 225&#160;million years ago), 40&#160;million years after the first therapsids. They expanded out of their nocturnal <a href="/wiki/Insectivore" title="Insectivore">insectivore</a> niche from the mid-Jurassic onwards;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the Jurassic <i><a href="/wiki/Castorocauda" title="Castorocauda">Castorocauda</a></i>, for example, was a close relative of true mammals that had adaptations for swimming, digging and catching fish.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-34" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most, if not all, are thought to have remained nocturnal (the <a href="/wiki/Nocturnal_bottleneck" title="Nocturnal bottleneck">nocturnal bottleneck</a>), accounting for much of the typical mammalian traits.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-35" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The majority of the mammal species that existed in the <a href="/wiki/Mesozoic" title="Mesozoic">Mesozoic Era</a> were multituberculates, eutriconodonts and <a href="/wiki/Spalacotheriid" class="mw-redirect" title="Spalacotheriid">spalacotheriids</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Luo2007&#95;36-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Luo2007-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The earliest-known fossil of the <a href="/wiki/Metatheria" title="Metatheria">Metatheria</a> ("changed beasts") is <i><a href="/wiki/Sinodelphys" title="Sinodelphys">Sinodelphys</a></i>, found in 125-million-year-old <a href="/wiki/Early_Cretaceous" title="Early Cretaceous">Early Cretaceous</a> <a href="/wiki/Shale" title="Shale">shale</a> in China's northeastern <a href="/wiki/Liaoning_Province" class="mw-redirect" title="Liaoning Province">Liaoning Province</a>. The fossil is nearly complete and includes tufts of fur and imprints of soft tissues.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-37" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Juramaia_NT.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Juramaia_NT.jpg/250px-Juramaia_NT.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="144" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/15/Juramaia_NT.jpg 2x" data-file-width="500" data-file-height="287" /></a><figcaption>Restoration of <i><a href="/wiki/Juramaia" title="Juramaia">Juramaia sinensis</a></i>, the oldest-known <a href="/wiki/Eutheria" title="Eutheria">eutherian</a> (160 mya)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Juramaia&#95;38-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Juramaia-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>The oldest-known fossil among the <a href="/wiki/Eutheria" title="Eutheria">Eutheria</a> ("true beasts") is the small shrewlike <i><a href="/wiki/Juramaia" title="Juramaia">Juramaia sinensis</a></i>, or "Jurassic mother from China", dated to 160&#160;million years ago in the late Jurassic.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Juramaia&#95;38-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Juramaia-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A later eutherian relative, <i><a href="/wiki/Eomaia" title="Eomaia">Eomaia</a></i>, dated to 125&#160;million years ago in the early Cretaceous, possessed some features in common with the marsupials but not with the placentals, evidence that these features were present in the last common ancestor of the two groups but were later lost in the placental lineage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-39" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In particular, the <a href="/wiki/Epipubic_bone" title="Epipubic bone">epipubic bones</a> extend forwards from the pelvis. These are not found in any modern placental, but they are found in marsupials, monotremes, other nontherian mammals and <i><a href="/wiki/Ukhaatherium" title="Ukhaatherium">Ukhaatherium</a></i>, an early Cretaceous animal in the eutherian order <a href="/wiki/Asioryctitheria" title="Asioryctitheria">Asioryctitheria</a>. This also applies to the multituberculates.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Epipubic&#95;bones&#95;in&#95;eutherian&#95;mammals&#95;40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Epipubic_bones_in_eutherian_mammals-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They are apparently an ancestral feature, which subsequently disappeared in the placental lineage. These epipubic bones seem to function by stiffening the muscles during locomotion, reducing the amount of space being presented, which placentals require to contain their <a href="/wiki/Fetus" title="Fetus">fetus</a> during gestation periods. A narrow pelvic outlet indicates that the young were very small at birth and therefore <a href="/wiki/Pregnancy_(mammals)" title="Pregnancy (mammals)">pregnancy</a> was short, as in modern marsupials. This suggests that the placenta was a later development.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-41" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>One of the earliest-known monotremes was <i><a href="/wiki/Teinolophos" title="Teinolophos">Teinolophos</a></i>, which lived about 120&#160;million years ago in Australia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-42" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Monotremes have some features which may be inherited from the original amniotes such as the same orifice to urinate, defecate and reproduce (<a href="/wiki/Cloaca" title="Cloaca">cloaca</a>)—as reptiles and birds also do—<sup id="cite&#95;ref-43" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and they lay <a href="/wiki/Egg_(biology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Egg (biology)">eggs</a> which are leathery and uncalcified.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Earliest_appearances_of_features">Earliest appearances of features</h3></div>
<p><i><a href="/wiki/Hadrocodium" title="Hadrocodium">Hadrocodium</a></i>, whose fossils date from approximately 195&#160;million years ago, in the early <a href="/wiki/Jurassic" title="Jurassic">Jurassic</a>, provides the first clear evidence of a jaw joint formed solely by the squamosal and dentary bones; there is no space in the jaw for the articular, a bone involved in the jaws of all early synapsids.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jawbone2006&#95;45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jawbone2006-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Thrinaxodon_Lionhinus.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Thrinaxodon_Lionhinus.jpg/250px-Thrinaxodon_Lionhinus.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="94" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Thrinaxodon_Lionhinus.jpg/500px-Thrinaxodon_Lionhinus.jpg 2x" data-file-width="840" data-file-height="315" /></a><figcaption>Fossil of <i><a href="/wiki/Thrinaxodon" title="Thrinaxodon">Thrinaxodon</a></i> at the <a href="/wiki/National_Museum_of_Natural_History" title="National Museum of Natural History">National Museum of Natural History</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>The earliest clear evidence of hair or fur is in fossils of <i><a href="/wiki/Castorocauda" title="Castorocauda">Castorocauda</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Megaconus" title="Megaconus">Megaconus</a></i>, from 164&#160;million years ago in the mid-Jurassic. In the 1950s, it was suggested that the foramina (passages) in the <a href="/wiki/Maxilla" title="Maxilla">maxillae</a> and <a href="/wiki/Premaxilla" title="Premaxilla">premaxillae</a> (bones in the front of the upper jaw) of cynodonts were channels which supplied blood vessels and nerves to vibrissae (<a href="/wiki/Whiskers" title="Whiskers">whiskers</a>) and so were evidence of hair or fur;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Brink1955&#95;46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Brink1955-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kemp1982&#95;47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kemp1982-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> it was soon pointed out, however, that foramina do not necessarily show that an animal had vibrissae, as the modern lizard <i><a href="/wiki/Tupinambis" title="Tupinambis">Tupinambis</a></i> has foramina that are almost identical to those found in the nonmammalian cynodont <i><a href="/wiki/Thrinaxodon" title="Thrinaxodon">Thrinaxodon</a></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bennett1986&#95;25-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bennett1986-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-48" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Popular sources, nevertheless, continue to attribute whiskers to <i>Thrinaxodon</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-49" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Studies on Permian <a href="/wiki/Coprolites" class="mw-redirect" title="Coprolites">coprolites</a> suggest that non-mammalian <a href="/wiki/Synapsids" class="mw-redirect" title="Synapsids">synapsids</a> of the epoch already had fur, setting the evolution of hairs possibly as far back as <a href="/wiki/Dicynodont" class="mw-redirect" title="Dicynodont">dicynodonts</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-piotr&#95;50-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-piotr-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>When <a href="/wiki/Endothermy" class="mw-redirect" title="Endothermy">endothermy</a> first appeared in the evolution of mammals is uncertain, though it is generally agreed to have first evolved in non-mammalian <a href="/wiki/Therapsids" class="mw-redirect" title="Therapsids">therapsids</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-piotr&#95;50-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-piotr-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-51" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Modern monotremes have lower body temperatures and more variable metabolic rates than marsupials and placentals,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Paul1988&#95;52-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Paul1988-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but there is evidence that some of their ancestors, perhaps including ancestors of the therians, may have had body temperatures like those of modern therians.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-53" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Likewise, some modern therians like afrotheres and xenarthrans have secondarily developed lower body temperatures.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-54" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The evolution of erect limbs in mammals is incomplete—living and fossil monotremes have sprawling limbs. The parasagittal (nonsprawling) limb posture appeared sometime in the late Jurassic or early Cretaceous; it is found in the eutherian <i>Eomaia</i> and the metatherian <i>Sinodelphys</i>, both dated to 125&#160;million years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-55" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Epipubic" class="mw-redirect" title="Epipubic">Epipubic</a> bones, a feature that strongly influenced the reproduction of most mammal clades, are first found in <a href="/wiki/Tritylodontidae" title="Tritylodontidae">Tritylodontidae</a>, suggesting that it is a <a href="/wiki/Synapomorphy" class="mw-redirect" title="Synapomorphy">synapomorphy</a> between them and <a href="/wiki/Mammaliaformes" title="Mammaliaformes">Mammaliaformes</a>. They are omnipresent in non-placental Mammaliaformes, though <i><a href="/wiki/Megazostrodon" title="Megazostrodon">Megazostrodon</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Erythrotherium" title="Erythrotherium">Erythrotherium</a></i> appear to have lacked them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-56" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>It has been suggested that the original function of <a href="/wiki/Lactation" title="Lactation">lactation</a> (<a href="/wiki/Milk" title="Milk">milk</a> production) was to keep eggs moist. Much of the argument is based on monotremes, the egg-laying mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-57" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-58" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-58"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In human females, mammary glands become fully developed during puberty, regardless of pregnancy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-59" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Rise_of_the_mammals">Rise of the mammals</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Hyaenodon_horridus,_Niobrara_County,_Wyoming,_USA,_Late_Oligocene_-_Royal_Ontario_Museum_-_DSC00114.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Hyaenodon_horridus%2C_Niobrara_County%2C_Wyoming%2C_USA%2C_Late_Oligocene_-_Royal_Ontario_Museum_-_DSC00114.JPG/250px-Hyaenodon_horridus%2C_Niobrara_County%2C_Wyoming%2C_USA%2C_Late_Oligocene_-_Royal_Ontario_Museum_-_DSC00114.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="128" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Hyaenodon_horridus%2C_Niobrara_County%2C_Wyoming%2C_USA%2C_Late_Oligocene_-_Royal_Ontario_Museum_-_DSC00114.JPG/500px-Hyaenodon_horridus%2C_Niobrara_County%2C_Wyoming%2C_USA%2C_Late_Oligocene_-_Royal_Ontario_Museum_-_DSC00114.JPG 2x" data-file-width="4136" data-file-height="2122" /></a><figcaption><i><a href="/wiki/Hyaenodon" title="Hyaenodon">Hyaenodon</a> horridus</i> at the <a href="/wiki/Royal_Ontario_Museum" title="Royal Ontario Museum">Royal Ontario Museum</a>. The genus <i>Hyaenodon</i> was among the most successful mammals of the late <a href="/wiki/Eocene" title="Eocene">Eocene</a>-early <a href="/wiki/Miocene" title="Miocene">Miocene</a> epochs spanning for most of the <a href="/wiki/Paleogene" title="Paleogene">Paleogene</a> and some of the <a href="/wiki/Neogene" title="Neogene">Neogene</a> periods, undergoing many endemic radiations in North America, Europe, and Asia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-60" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Therians took over the medium- to large-sized ecological niches in the <a href="/wiki/Cenozoic" title="Cenozoic">Cenozoic</a>, after the <a href="/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event" title="Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event">Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event</a> approximately 66&#160;million years ago emptied ecological space once filled by non-avian dinosaurs and other groups of reptiles, as well as various other mammal groups,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SahneyBentonFerry2010LinksDiversityVertebrates&#95;61-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SahneyBentonFerry2010LinksDiversityVertebrates-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and underwent an exponential increase in body size (<a href="/wiki/Megafauna#In_terrestrial_mammals" title="Megafauna">megafauna</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-F.A.Smith&#95;62-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-F.A.Smith-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The increase in mammalian diversity was not, however, solely because of expansion into large-bodied niches.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-63" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals diversified very quickly, displaying an exponential rise in diversity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SahneyBentonFerry2010LinksDiversityVertebrates&#95;61-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SahneyBentonFerry2010LinksDiversityVertebrates-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, the earliest-known bat dates from about 50&#160;million years ago, only 16&#160;million years after the extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-64" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Molecular phylogenetic studies initially suggested that most placental orders diverged about 100 to 85&#160;million years ago and that modern families appeared in the period from the late <a href="/wiki/Eocene" title="Eocene">Eocene</a> through the <a href="/wiki/Miocene" title="Miocene">Miocene</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bininda2007&#95;65-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bininda2007-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, no placental fossils have been found from before the end of the Cretaceous.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Wible2007&#95;66-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Wible2007-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The earliest undisputed fossils of placentals come from the early <a href="/wiki/Paleocene" title="Paleocene">Paleocene</a>, after the extinction of the non-avian dinosaurs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Wible2007&#95;66-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Wible2007-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> (Scientists identified an early Paleocene animal named <i><a href="/wiki/Protungulatum_donnae" class="mw-redirect" title="Protungulatum donnae">Protungulatum donnae</a></i> as one of the first placental mammals,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SCI-20130208&#95;67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SCI-20130208-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but it has since been reclassified as a non-placental eutherian.)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-68" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Recalibrations of genetic and morphological diversity rates have suggested a <a href="/wiki/Maastrichtian" title="Maastrichtian">Late Cretaceous</a> origin for placentals, and a Paleocene origin for most modern clades.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-69" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The earliest-known ancestor of primates is <i><a href="/wiki/Archicebus_achilles" class="mw-redirect" title="Archicebus achilles">Archicebus achilles</a></i><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NTR-20130606&#95;70-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NTR-20130606-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> from around 55&#160;million years ago.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NTR-20130606&#95;70-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NTR-20130606-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This tiny primate weighed 20–30 grams (0.7–1.1 ounce) and could fit within a human palm.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NTR-20130606&#95;70-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NTR-20130606-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><span class="anchor" id="Anatomy&#95;and&#95;morphology"></span>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Anatomy">Anatomy</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Distinguishing_features">Distinguishing features</h3></div>
<p>Living mammal species can be identified by the presence of <a href="/wiki/Sweat_gland" title="Sweat gland">sweat glands</a>, including <a href="/wiki/Mammary_gland" title="Mammary gland">those that are specialised to produce milk</a> to nourish their young.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-71" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In classifying fossils, however, other features must be used, since soft tissue glands and many other features are not visible in fossils.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-72" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many traits shared by all living mammals appeared among the earliest members of the group:
</p>
<ul><li><b><a href="/wiki/Jaw" title="Jaw">Jaw joint</a></b> – The <a href="/wiki/Dentary" class="mw-redirect" title="Dentary">dentary</a> (the lower jaw bone, which carries the teeth) and the <a href="/wiki/Squamosal" class="mw-redirect" title="Squamosal">squamosal</a> (a small <a href="/wiki/Cranial" class="mw-redirect" title="Cranial">cranial</a> bone) meet to form the joint. In most <a href="/wiki/Gnathostomes" class="mw-redirect" title="Gnathostomes">gnathostomes</a>, including early <a href="/wiki/Therapsids" class="mw-redirect" title="Therapsids">therapsids</a>, the joint consists of the <a href="/wiki/Articular" class="mw-redirect" title="Articular">articular</a> (a small bone at the back of the lower jaw) and <a href="/wiki/Quadrate_bone" title="Quadrate bone">quadrate</a> (a small bone at the back of the upper jaw).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jawbone2006&#95;45-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jawbone2006-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Middle_ear" title="Middle ear">Middle ear</a></b> – In crown-group mammals, sound is carried from the <a href="/wiki/Eardrum" title="Eardrum">eardrum</a> by a chain of three bones, the <a href="/wiki/Malleus" title="Malleus">malleus</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Incus" title="Incus">incus</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Stapes" title="Stapes">stapes</a>. Ancestrally, the malleus and the incus are derived from the articular and the quadrate bones that constituted the jaw joint of early therapsids.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-73" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><b>Tooth replacement</b> – Teeth can be replaced once (<a href="/wiki/Diphyodonty" class="mw-redirect" title="Diphyodonty">diphyodonty</a>) or (as in toothed whales and <a href="/wiki/Muridae" title="Muridae">murid</a> rodents) not at all (<a href="/wiki/Dentition" title="Dentition">monophyodonty</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-74" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Elephants, manatees, and kangaroos continually grow new teeth throughout their life (<a href="/wiki/Polyphyodont" title="Polyphyodont">polyphyodonty</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-75" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><b>Prismatic enamel</b> – The <a href="/wiki/Tooth_enamel" title="Tooth enamel">enamel</a> coating on the surface of a tooth consists of prisms, solid, rod-like structures extending from the <a href="/wiki/Dentin" title="Dentin">dentin</a> to the tooth's surface.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-76" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><b><a href="/wiki/Occipital_condyle" class="mw-redirect" title="Occipital condyle">Occipital condyles</a></b> – Two knobs at the base of the skull fit into the topmost <a href="/wiki/Cervical_vertebrae" title="Cervical vertebrae">neck vertebra</a>; most other <a href="/wiki/Tetrapod" title="Tetrapod">tetrapods</a>, in contrast, have only one such knob.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-77" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<p>For the most part, these characteristics were not present in the Triassic ancestors of the mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-78" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Nearly all mammaliaforms possess an epipubic bone, the exception being modern placentals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-schulkin&#95;79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-schulkin-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sexual_dimorphism">Sexual dimorphism</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Aurochsfeatures.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Aurochsfeatures.jpg/250px-Aurochsfeatures.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="186" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Aurochsfeatures.jpg/500px-Aurochsfeatures.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1041" data-file-height="776" /></a><figcaption>Sexual dimorphism in <a href="/wiki/Aurochs" title="Aurochs">aurochs</a>, the extinct wild ancestor of <a href="/wiki/Cattle" title="Cattle">cattle</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>On average, male mammals are larger than females, with males being at least 10% larger than females in over 45% of investigated species. Most mammalian orders also exhibit male-biased <a href="/wiki/Sexual_dimorphism" title="Sexual dimorphism">sexual dimorphism</a>, although some orders do not show any bias or are significantly female-biased (<a href="/wiki/Lagomorpha" title="Lagomorpha">Lagomorpha</a>). Sexual size dimorphism increases with body size across mammals (<a href="/wiki/Rensch%27s_rule" title="Rensch&#39;s rule">Rensch's rule</a>), suggesting that there are parallel selection pressures on both male and female size. Male-biased dimorphism <a href="/wiki/Sexual_selection_in_mammals" title="Sexual selection in mammals">relates to sexual selection</a> on males through male–male competition for females, as there is a positive correlation between the degree of sexual selection, as indicated by <a href="/wiki/Mating_system" title="Mating system">mating systems</a>, and the degree of male-biased size dimorphism. The degree of sexual selection is also positively correlated with male and female size across mammals. Further, parallel selection pressure on female mass is identified in that age at weaning is significantly higher in more <a href="/wiki/Polygyny_in_animals" title="Polygyny in animals">polygynous</a> species, even when correcting for body mass. Also, the reproductive rate is lower for larger females, indicating that fecundity selection selects for smaller females in mammals. Although these patterns hold across mammals as a whole, there is considerable variation across orders.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-80" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Biological_systems">Biological systems</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Biological_system" title="Biological system">Biological system</a></div>
<p>The majority of mammals have seven <a href="/wiki/Cervical_vertebrae" title="Cervical vertebrae">cervical vertebrae</a> (bones in the neck). The exceptions are the <a href="/wiki/Manatee" title="Manatee">manatee</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Two-toed_sloth" title="Two-toed sloth">two-toed sloth</a>, which have six, and the <a href="/wiki/Three-toed_sloth" title="Three-toed sloth">three-toed sloth</a> which has nine.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-81" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> All mammalian brains possess a <a href="/wiki/Neocortex" title="Neocortex">neocortex</a>, a brain region unique to mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-82" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Placental brains have a <a href="/wiki/Corpus_callosum" title="Corpus callosum">corpus callosum</a>, unlike monotremes and marsupials.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-83" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Didactic_model_of_a_mammal_heart_04--FMVZ_USP-11.jpeg/500px-Didactic_model_of_a_mammal_heart_04--FMVZ_USP-11.jpeg 2x" data-file-width="3872" data-file-height="2592" /></a></span></div></div></div><div class="trow" style="display:flow-root"><div class="thumbcaption" style="text-align:center"><a href="/wiki/Educational_toy" title="Educational toy">Didactic models</a> of a mammalian heart</div></div></div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Circulatory_systems">Circulatory systems</h4></div>
<p>The mammalian <a href="/wiki/Heart" title="Heart">heart</a> has four chambers, two upper <a href="/wiki/Atrium_(heart)" title="Atrium (heart)">atria</a>, the receiving chambers, and two lower <a href="/wiki/Ventricle_(heart)" title="Ventricle (heart)">ventricles</a>, the discharging chambers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-84" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The heart has four valves, which separate its chambers and ensures blood flows in the correct direction through the heart (preventing backflow). After <a href="/wiki/Gas_exchange" title="Gas exchange">gas exchange</a> in the pulmonary capillaries (blood vessels in the lungs), oxygen-rich blood returns to the left atrium via one of the four <a href="/wiki/Pulmonary_vein" title="Pulmonary vein">pulmonary veins</a>. Blood flows nearly continuously back into the atrium, which acts as the receiving chamber, and from here through an opening into the left ventricle. Most blood flows passively into the heart while both the atria and ventricles are relaxed, but toward the end of the <a href="/wiki/Diastole" title="Diastole">ventricular relaxation period</a>, the left atrium will contract, pumping blood into the ventricle. The heart also requires nutrients and oxygen found in blood like other muscles, and is supplied via <a href="/wiki/Coronary_circulation" title="Coronary circulation">coronary arteries</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-85" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
