The <a href="/wiki/Red_blood_cell" title="Red blood cell">red blood cells</a> of mammals also lack a nucleus to make space for more <a href="/wiki/Haemoglobin" class="mw-redirect" title="Haemoglobin">haemoglobin</a>, which significantly increases the cell's oxygen-carrying capacity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-86" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Respiratory_systems">Respiratory systems</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Lung_expansion_simulation_with_Raccoon.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Lung_expansion_simulation_with_Raccoon.gif/250px-Lung_expansion_simulation_with_Raccoon.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="161" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Lung_expansion_simulation_with_Raccoon.gif/500px-Lung_expansion_simulation_with_Raccoon.gif 2x" data-file-width="620" data-file-height="399" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Raccoon" title="Raccoon">Raccoon</a> lungs being inflated manually</figcaption></figure>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Respiratory_system#Mammals" title="Respiratory system">Respiratory system §&#160;Mammals</a></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Lung" title="Lung">lungs</a> of mammals are spongy and honeycombed. Breathing is mainly achieved with the <a href="/wiki/Diaphragm_(anatomy)" class="mw-redirect" title="Diaphragm (anatomy)">diaphragm</a>, which divides the thorax from the abdominal cavity, forming a dome convex to the thorax. Contraction of the diaphragm flattens the dome, increasing the volume of the lung cavity. Air enters through the oral and nasal cavities, and travels through the larynx, trachea and <a href="/wiki/Bronchi" class="mw-redirect" title="Bronchi">bronchi</a>, and expands the <a href="/wiki/Pulmonary_alveolus" title="Pulmonary alveolus">alveoli</a>. Relaxing the diaphragm has the opposite effect, decreasing the volume of the lung cavity, causing air to be pushed out of the lungs. During exercise, the abdominal wall <a href="/wiki/Muscle_contraction" title="Muscle contraction">contracts</a>, increasing pressure on the diaphragm, which forces air out quicker and more forcefully. The <a href="/wiki/Rib_cage" title="Rib cage">rib cage</a> is able to expand and contract the chest cavity through the action of other respiratory muscles. Consequently, air is sucked into or expelled out of the lungs, always moving down its pressure gradient.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-bellows&#95;88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bellows-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This type of lung is known as a bellows lung due to its resemblance to blacksmith <a href="/wiki/Bellows" title="Bellows">bellows</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bellows&#95;88-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bellows-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
<br />
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Integumentary_systems">Integumentary systems</h4></div>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_skin_of_mammals.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/The_skin_of_mammals.jpg/250px-The_skin_of_mammals.jpg" decoding="async" width="235" height="299" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/The_skin_of_mammals.jpg/500px-The_skin_of_mammals.jpg 2x" data-file-width="550" data-file-height="700" /></a><figcaption>Mammal skin: (1) <a href="/wiki/Hair" title="Hair">hair</a>, (2) <a href="/wiki/Epidermis" title="Epidermis">epidermis</a>, (3) <a href="/wiki/Sebaceous_gland" title="Sebaceous gland">sebaceous gland</a>, (4) <a href="/wiki/Arrector_pili_muscle" title="Arrector pili muscle">Arrector pili muscle</a>, (5) <a href="/wiki/Dermis" title="Dermis">dermis</a>, (6) <a href="/wiki/Hair_follicle" title="Hair follicle">hair follicle</a>, (7) <a href="/wiki/Sweat_gland" title="Sweat gland">sweat gland</a>. Not labelled, the bottom layer: <a href="/wiki/Hypodermis" class="mw-redirect" title="Hypodermis">hypodermis</a>, showing round <a href="/wiki/Adipocytes" class="mw-redirect" title="Adipocytes">adipocytes</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Skin" title="Skin">integumentary system</a> (skin) is made up of three layers: the outermost <a href="/wiki/Epidermis_(skin)" class="mw-redirect" title="Epidermis (skin)">epidermis</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Dermis" title="Dermis">dermis</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Hypodermis" class="mw-redirect" title="Hypodermis">hypodermis</a>. The epidermis is typically 10 to 30 cells thick; its main function is to provide a waterproof layer. Its outermost cells are constantly lost; its bottommost cells are constantly dividing and pushing upward. The middle layer, the dermis, is 15 to 40 times thicker than the epidermis. The dermis is made up of many components, such as bony structures and blood vessels. The hypodermis is made up of <a href="/wiki/Adipose_tissue" title="Adipose tissue">adipose tissue</a>, which stores lipids and provides cushioning and insulation. The thickness of this layer varies widely from species to species;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 97">&#58;&#8202;97&#8202;</span></sup> <a href="/wiki/Marine_mammal" title="Marine mammal">marine mammals</a> require a thick hypodermis (<a href="/wiki/Blubber" title="Blubber">blubber</a>) for insulation, and <a href="/wiki/Right_whale" title="Right whale">right whales</a> have the thickest blubber at 20 inches (51&#160;cm).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-90" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Although other animals have features such as whiskers, <a href="/wiki/Feather" title="Feather">feathers</a>, <a href="/wiki/Setae" class="mw-redirect" title="Setae">setae</a>, or <a href="/wiki/Cilia_(entomology)" class="mw-redirect" title="Cilia (entomology)">cilia</a> that superficially resemble it, no animals other than mammals have <a href="/wiki/Hair" title="Hair">hair</a>. It is a definitive characteristic of the class, though some mammals have very little.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 61">&#58;&#8202;61&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Digestive_systems">Digestive systems</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:408px;max-width:408px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:202px;max-width:202px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Aardwolfskull.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Aardwolfskull.jpg/250px-Aardwolfskull.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/1/16/Aardwolfskull.jpg 2x" data-file-width="402" data-file-height="284" /></a></span></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:202px;max-width:202px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Canis_lupus_02_MWNH_358.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Canis_lupus_02_MWNH_358.jpg/250px-Canis_lupus_02_MWNH_358.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="133" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Canis_lupus_02_MWNH_358.jpg/500px-Canis_lupus_02_MWNH_358.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a></span></div></div></div><div class="trow" style="display:flex"><div class="thumbcaption">The <a href="/wiki/Carnassial" title="Carnassial">carnassials</a> (teeth in the very back of the mouth) of the <a href="/wiki/Insectivorous" class="mw-redirect" title="Insectivorous">insectivorous</a> <a href="/wiki/Aardwolf" title="Aardwolf">aardwolf</a> (left) versus that of a <a href="/wiki/Grey_wolf" class="mw-redirect" title="Grey wolf">grey wolf</a> (right) which consumes large vertebrates</div></div></div></div>
<p>Herbivores have developed a diverse range of physical structures to facilitate the <a href="/wiki/Herbivore_adaptations_to_plant_defense" title="Herbivore adaptations to plant defense">consumption of plant material</a>. To break up intact plant tissues, mammals have developed <a href="/wiki/Mammal_tooth" title="Mammal tooth">teeth</a> structures that reflect their feeding preferences. For instance, <a href="/wiki/Frugivore" title="Frugivore">frugivores</a> (animals that feed primarily on fruit) and herbivores that feed on soft foliage have low-crowned teeth specialised for grinding foliage and <a href="/wiki/Seed" title="Seed">seeds</a>. <a href="/wiki/Grazing" title="Grazing">Grazing</a> animals that tend to eat hard, <a href="/wiki/Silica" class="mw-redirect" title="Silica">silica</a>-rich grasses, have high-crowned teeth, which are capable of grinding tough plant tissues and do not wear down as quickly as low-crowned teeth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-91" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most carnivorous mammals have <a href="/wiki/Carnassial" title="Carnassial">carnassial</a> teeth (of varying length depending on diet), long canines and similar tooth replacement patterns.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-92" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The stomach of <a href="/wiki/Even-toed_ungulates" class="mw-redirect" title="Even-toed ungulates">even-toed ungulates</a> (Artiodactyla) is divided into four sections: the <a href="/wiki/Rumen" title="Rumen">rumen</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Reticulum_(anatomy)" title="Reticulum (anatomy)">reticulum</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Omasum" title="Omasum">omasum</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Abomasum" title="Abomasum">abomasum</a> (only <a href="/wiki/Ruminant" title="Ruminant">ruminants</a> have a rumen). After the plant material is consumed, it is mixed with saliva in the rumen and reticulum and separates into solid and liquid material. The solids lump together to form a <a href="/wiki/Bolus_(digestion)" class="mw-redirect" title="Bolus (digestion)">bolus</a> (or <a href="/wiki/Cud" title="Cud">cud</a>), and is regurgitated. When the bolus enters the mouth, the fluid is squeezed out with the tongue and swallowed again. Ingested food passes to the rumen and reticulum where cellulolytic <a href="/wiki/Microbe" class="mw-redirect" title="Microbe">microbes</a> (<a href="/wiki/Bacterium" class="mw-redirect" title="Bacterium">bacteria</a>, <a href="/wiki/Protozoa" title="Protozoa">protozoa</a> and <a href="/wiki/Fungus" title="Fungus">fungi</a>) produce <a href="/wiki/Cellulase" title="Cellulase">cellulase</a>, which is needed to break down the <a href="/wiki/Cellulose" title="Cellulose">cellulose</a> in plants.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Comparative&#95;anatomy&#95;of&#95;the&#95;stomach&#95;93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Comparative_anatomy_of_the_stomach-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Perissodactyls" class="mw-redirect" title="Perissodactyls">Perissodactyls</a>, in contrast to the ruminants, store digested food that has left the stomach in an enlarged <a href="/wiki/Cecum" title="Cecum">cecum</a>, where it is fermented by bacteria.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-94" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Carnivora have a simple stomach adapted to digest primarily meat, as compared to the elaborate digestive systems of herbivorous animals, which are necessary to break down tough, complex plant fibres. The cecum is either absent or short and simple, and the large intestine is not <a href="/wiki/Sacculation" class="mw-redirect" title="Sacculation">sacculated</a> or much wider than the small intestine.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-95" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Excretory_and_genitourinary_systems">Excretory and genitourinary systems</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Glycerination_of_Bovine_kidney.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Glycerination_of_Bovine_kidney.jpg/250px-Glycerination_of_Bovine_kidney.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Glycerination_of_Bovine_kidney.jpg/500px-Glycerination_of_Bovine_kidney.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3872" data-file-height="2592" /></a><figcaption>Bovine kidney</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Image_from_page_702_of_%22Outlines_of_zoology%22_(1895)_(20732795545).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Image_from_page_702_of_%22Outlines_of_zoology%22_%281895%29_%2820732795545%29.jpg/250px-Image_from_page_702_of_%22Outlines_of_zoology%22_%281895%29_%2820732795545%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="192" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Image_from_page_702_of_%22Outlines_of_zoology%22_%281895%29_%2820732795545%29.jpg/500px-Image_from_page_702_of_%22Outlines_of_zoology%22_%281895%29_%2820732795545%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1076" data-file-height="1090" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Genitourinary_system" title="Genitourinary system">Genitourinary system</a> of a male and female rabbit</figcaption></figure>
<p>The mammalian <a href="/wiki/Excretory_system" title="Excretory system">excretory system</a> involves many components. Like amphibians, mammals are <a href="/wiki/Ureotelic" class="mw-redirect" title="Ureotelic">ureotelic</a>, and convert <a href="/wiki/Ammonia" title="Ammonia">ammonia</a> into <a href="/wiki/Urea" title="Urea">urea</a>, which is done by the <a href="/wiki/Liver" title="Liver">liver</a> as part of the <a href="/wiki/Urea_cycle" title="Urea cycle">urea cycle</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Bilirubin" title="Bilirubin">Bilirubin</a>, a waste product derived from <a href="/wiki/Blood_cell" title="Blood cell">blood cells</a>, is passed through <a href="/wiki/Bile" title="Bile">bile</a> and <a href="/wiki/Urine" title="Urine">urine</a> with the help of enzymes excreted by the liver.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-97" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The passing of bilirubin via bile through the <a href="/wiki/Intestinal_tract" class="mw-redirect" title="Intestinal tract">intestinal tract</a> gives mammalian <a href="/wiki/Feces" title="Feces">feces</a> a distinctive brown coloration.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-98" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Distinctive features of the <a href="/wiki/Mammalian_kidney" title="Mammalian kidney">mammalian kidney</a> include the presence of the <a href="/wiki/Renal_pelvis" title="Renal pelvis">renal pelvis</a> and <a href="/wiki/Renal_pyramid" class="mw-redirect" title="Renal pyramid">renal pyramids</a>, and of a clearly distinguishable <a href="/wiki/Renal_cortex" title="Renal cortex">cortex</a> and <a href="/wiki/Renal_medulla" title="Renal medulla">medulla</a>, which is due to the presence of elongated <a href="/wiki/Loop_of_Henle" title="Loop of Henle">loops of Henle</a>. Only the mammalian kidney has a bean shape, although there are some exceptions, such as the multilobed <a href="/wiki/Reniculate_kidney" title="Reniculate kidney">reniculate kidneys</a> of pinnipeds, <a href="/wiki/Cetacea" class="mw-redirect" title="Cetacea">cetaceans</a> and bears.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-99" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-99"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>99<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-VB&#95;100-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-VB-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most adult placentals have no remaining trace of the <a href="/wiki/Cloaca" title="Cloaca">cloaca</a>. In the embryo, the <a href="/wiki/Embryonic_cloaca" class="mw-redirect" title="Embryonic cloaca">embryonic cloaca</a> divides into a posterior region that becomes part of the <a href="/wiki/Anus" title="Anus">anus</a>, and an anterior region that has different fates depending on the sex of the individual: in females, it develops into the <a href="/wiki/Vulval_vestibule" title="Vulval vestibule">vestibule</a> or <a href="/wiki/Urogenital_sinus#Other_animals" title="Urogenital sinus">urogenital sinus</a> that receives the <a href="/wiki/Urethra" title="Urethra">urethra</a> and <a href="/wiki/Vagina" title="Vagina">vagina</a>, while in males it forms the entirety of the <a href="/wiki/Penile_urethra" class="mw-redirect" title="Penile urethra">penile urethra</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-VB&#95;100-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-VB-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-101" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-101"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, the <a href="/wiki/Afrosoricida" title="Afrosoricida">afrosoricids</a> and some <a href="/wiki/Shrew" title="Shrew">shrews</a> retain a cloaca as adults.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-102" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In marsupials, the genital tract is separate from the anus, but a trace of the original cloaca does remain externally.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-VB&#95;100-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-VB-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Monotremes, which translates from <a href="/wiki/Ancient_Greek" title="Ancient Greek">Greek</a> into "single hole", have a true cloaca.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Urine flows from the <a href="/wiki/Ureter" title="Ureter">ureters</a> into the cloaca in monotremes and into the <a href="/wiki/Bladder" title="Bladder">bladder</a> in placentals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-VB&#95;100-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-VB-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Sound_production">Sound production</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Animal_echolocation.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Animal_echolocation.svg/500px-Animal_echolocation.svg.png" decoding="async" width="340" height="262" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Animal_echolocation.svg/960px-Animal_echolocation.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="318" data-file-height="245" /></a><figcaption>A diagram of ultrasonic signals emitted by a bat, and the echo from a nearby object</figcaption></figure>
<p>As in all other tetrapods, mammals have a <a href="/wiki/Larynx" title="Larynx">larynx</a> that can quickly open and close to produce sounds, and a supralaryngeal <a href="/wiki/Vocal_tract" title="Vocal tract">vocal tract</a> which filters this sound. The lungs and surrounding musculature provide the air stream and pressure required to <a href="/wiki/Phonate" class="mw-redirect" title="Phonate">phonate</a>. The larynx controls the <a href="/wiki/Pitch_(music)" title="Pitch (music)">pitch</a> and <a href="/wiki/Loudness" title="Loudness">volume</a> of sound, but the strength the lungs exert to <a href="/wiki/Exhale" class="mw-redirect" title="Exhale">exhale</a> also contributes to volume. More primitive mammals, such as the echidna, can only hiss, as sound is achieved solely through exhaling through a partially closed larynx. Other mammals phonate using <a href="/wiki/Vocal_fold" class="mw-redirect" title="Vocal fold">vocal folds</a>. The movement or tenseness of the vocal folds can result in many sounds such as <a href="/wiki/Purr" title="Purr">purring</a> and <a href="/wiki/Screaming" title="Screaming">screaming</a>. Mammals can change the position of the larynx, allowing them to breathe through the nose while swallowing through the mouth, and to form both oral and <a href="/wiki/Nasalization" title="Nasalization">nasal</a> sounds; nasal sounds, such as a dog whine, are generally soft sounds, and oral sounds, such as a dog bark, are generally loud.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-fitchbrown2006&#95;104-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fitchbrown2006-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><audio id="mwe_player_0" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" style="width:250px;" data-durationhint="17" data-mwtitle="Beluga_vocalizations.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Beluga_vocalizations.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9f/Beluga_vocalizations.ogg" type="audio/ogg; codecs=&quot;vorbis&quot;" data-width="0" data-height="0" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/9f/Beluga_vocalizations.ogg/Beluga_vocalizations.ogg.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" data-transcodekey="mp3" data-width="0" data-height="0" /></audio></span><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Beluga_whale" title="Beluga whale">Beluga whale</a> echolocation sounds</figcaption></figure>
<p>Some mammals have a large larynx and thus a low-pitched voice, namely the <a href="/wiki/Hammer-headed_bat" title="Hammer-headed bat">hammer-headed bat</a> (<i>Hypsignathus monstrosus</i>) where the larynx can take up the entirety of the <a href="/wiki/Thoracic_cavity" title="Thoracic cavity">thoracic cavity</a> while pushing the lungs, heart, and trachea into the <a href="/wiki/Abdomen" title="Abdomen">abdomen</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-105" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Large vocal pads can also lower the pitch, as in the low-pitched roars of <a href="/wiki/Big_cat" title="Big cat">big cats</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-106" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The production of <a href="/wiki/Infrasound" title="Infrasound">infrasound</a> is possible in some mammals such as the <a href="/wiki/African_elephant" title="African elephant">African elephant</a> (<i>Loxodonta</i> spp.) and <a href="/wiki/Baleen_whale" title="Baleen whale">baleen whales</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-107" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-108" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Small mammals with small larynxes have the ability to produce <a href="/wiki/Ultrasound" title="Ultrasound">ultrasound</a>, which can be detected by modifications to the <a href="/wiki/Middle_ear" title="Middle ear">middle ear</a> and <a href="/wiki/Cochlea" title="Cochlea">cochlea</a>. Ultrasound is inaudible to birds and reptiles, which might have been important during the Mesozoic, when birds and reptiles were the dominant predators. This private channel is used by some rodents in, for example, mother-to-pup communication, and by bats when echolocating. Toothed whales also use echolocation, but, as opposed to the vocal membrane that extends upward from the vocal folds, they have a <a href="/wiki/Melon_(cetacean)" title="Melon (cetacean)">melon</a> to manipulate sounds. Some mammals, namely the primates, have air sacs attached to the larynx, which may function to lower the resonances or increase the volume of sound.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-fitchbrown2006&#95;104-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fitchbrown2006-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The vocal production system is controlled by the <a href="/wiki/Cranial_nerve_nucleus" title="Cranial nerve nucleus">cranial nerve nuclei</a> in the brain, and supplied by the <a href="/wiki/Recurrent_laryngeal_nerve" title="Recurrent laryngeal nerve">recurrent laryngeal nerve</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Superior_laryngeal_nerve" title="Superior laryngeal nerve">superior laryngeal nerve</a>, branches of the <a href="/wiki/Vagus_nerve" title="Vagus nerve">vagus nerve</a>. The vocal tract is supplied by the <a href="/wiki/Hypoglossal_nerve" title="Hypoglossal nerve">hypoglossal nerve</a> and <a href="/wiki/Facial_nerve" title="Facial nerve">facial nerves</a>. Electrical stimulation of the <a href="/wiki/Periaqueductal_grey" class="mw-redirect" title="Periaqueductal grey">periaqueductal grey</a> (PEG) region of the mammalian <a href="/wiki/Midbrain" title="Midbrain">midbrain</a> elicit vocalisations. The ability to learn new vocalisations is only exemplified in humans, seals, cetaceans, elephants and possibly bats; in humans, this is the result of a direct connection between the <a href="/wiki/Motor_cortex" title="Motor cortex">motor cortex</a>, which controls movement, and the <a href="/wiki/Motor_neuron" title="Motor neuron">motor neurons</a> in the spinal cord.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-fitchbrown2006&#95;104-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-fitchbrown2006-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Fur">Fur</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Fur" title="Fur">Fur</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Stekelvarken_Aiguilles_Porc-%C3%A9pic.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Stekelvarken_Aiguilles_Porc-%C3%A9pic.jpg/250px-Stekelvarken_Aiguilles_Porc-%C3%A9pic.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="110" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Stekelvarken_Aiguilles_Porc-%C3%A9pic.jpg/500px-Stekelvarken_Aiguilles_Porc-%C3%A9pic.jpg 2x" data-file-width="613" data-file-height="269" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Porcupine" title="Porcupine">Porcupines</a> use their <a href="/wiki/Spine_(zoology)" title="Spine (zoology)">spines</a> for defence.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The primary function of the fur of mammals is <a href="/wiki/Thermoregulation" title="Thermoregulation">thermoregulation</a>. Others include protection, sensory purposes, waterproofing, and camouflage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-dawson2014&#95;109-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dawson2014-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Different types of fur serve different purposes:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 99">&#58;&#8202;99&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<ul><li>Definitive – which may be <a href="/wiki/Moulting" title="Moulting">shed</a> after reaching a certain length</li>
<li>Vibrissae – sensory hairs, most commonly <a href="/wiki/Whisker" class="mw-redirect" title="Whisker">whiskers</a></li>
<li>Pelage – guard hairs, under-fur, and <a href="/wiki/Awn_hair" title="Awn hair">awn hair</a></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Spine_(zoology)" title="Spine (zoology)">Spines</a> – stiff guard hair used for defence (such as in <a href="/wiki/Porcupine" title="Porcupine">porcupines</a>)</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Bristle" title="Bristle">Bristles</a> – long hairs usually used in visual signals. (such as a lion's <a href="/wiki/Mane_(lion)" class="mw-redirect" title="Mane (lion)">mane</a>)</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Vellus_hair" title="Vellus hair">Velli</a> – often called "down fur" which insulates newborn mammals</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Wool" title="Wool">Wool</a> – long, soft and often curly</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Thermoregulation">Thermoregulation</h4></div>
<p>Hair length is not a factor in thermoregulation: for example, some tropical mammals such as sloths have the same length of fur length as some arctic mammals but with less insulation; and, conversely, other tropical mammals with short hair have the same insulating value as arctic mammals. The denseness of fur can increase an animal's insulation value, and arctic mammals especially have dense fur; for example, the <a href="/wiki/Musk_ox" class="mw-redirect" title="Musk ox">musk ox</a> has guard hairs measuring 30&#160;cm (12&#160;in) as well as a dense underfur, which forms an airtight coat, allowing them to survive in temperatures of −40&#160;°C (−40&#160;°F).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 162–163">&#58;&#8202;162–163&#8202;</span></sup> Some desert mammals, such as camels, use dense fur to prevent solar heat from reaching their skin, allowing the animal to stay cool; a camel's fur may reach 70&#160;°C (158&#160;°F) in the summer, but the skin stays at 40&#160;°C (104&#160;°F).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 188">&#58;&#8202;188&#8202;</span></sup> <a href="/wiki/Aquatic_mammal" title="Aquatic mammal">Aquatic mammals</a>, conversely, trap air in their fur to conserve heat by keeping the skin dry.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 162–163">&#58;&#8202;162–163&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Great_male_Leopard_in_South_Afrika-JD.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Great_male_Leopard_in_South_Afrika-JD.JPG/250px-Great_male_Leopard_in_South_Afrika-JD.JPG" decoding="async" width="250" height="175" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Great_male_Leopard_in_South_Afrika-JD.JPG/500px-Great_male_Leopard_in_South_Afrika-JD.JPG 2x" data-file-width="2000" data-file-height="1400" /></a><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Leopard" title="Leopard">leopard</a>'s <a href="/wiki/Disruptive_coloration" title="Disruptive coloration">disruptively coloured</a> coat provides <a href="/wiki/Camouflage" title="Camouflage">camouflage</a> for this <a href="/wiki/Ambush_predator" title="Ambush predator">ambush predator</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Coloration">Coloration</h4></div>
<p>Mammalian coats are coloured for a variety of reasons, the major selective pressures including <a href="/wiki/Camouflage" title="Camouflage">camouflage</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sexual_selection" title="Sexual selection">sexual selection</a>, communication, and thermoregulation. Coloration in both the hair and skin of mammals is mainly determined by the type and amount of <a href="/wiki/Melanin" title="Melanin">melanin</a>; <a href="/wiki/Eumelanin" class="mw-redirect" title="Eumelanin">eumelanins</a> for brown and black colours and <a href="/wiki/Pheomelanin" class="mw-redirect" title="Pheomelanin">pheomelanin</a> for a range of yellowish to reddish colours, giving mammals an <a href="/wiki/Earth_tone" title="Earth tone">earth tone</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-110" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-HiltonPond&#95;111-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HiltonPond-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some mammals have more vibrant colours; certain monkeys such <a href="/wiki/Mandrill" title="Mandrill">mandrills</a> and <a href="/wiki/Vervet_monkey" title="Vervet monkey">vervet monkeys</a>, and opossums such as the <a href="/wiki/Mexican_mouse_opossum" title="Mexican mouse opossum">Mexican mouse opossums</a> and <a href="/wiki/Derby%27s_woolly_opossum" title="Derby&#39;s woolly opossum">Derby's woolly opossums</a>, have blue skin due to <a href="/wiki/Structural_coloration" title="Structural coloration">light diffraction</a> in <a href="/wiki/Collagen" title="Collagen">collagen</a> fibres.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prum2004&#95;112-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prum2004-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many sloths appear green because their fur hosts green <a href="/wiki/Algae" title="Algae">algae</a>; this may be a <a href="/wiki/Symbiosis" title="Symbiosis">symbiotic</a> relation that affords camouflage to the sloths.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-113" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Camouflage is a powerful influence in a large number of mammals, as it helps to conceal individuals from predators or prey.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bioscience.oxfordjournals.org&#95;114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bioscience.oxfordjournals.org-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In arctic and subarctic mammals such as the <a href="/wiki/Arctic_fox" title="Arctic fox">arctic fox</a> (<i>Alopex lagopus</i>), <a href="/wiki/Collared_lemming" title="Collared lemming">collared lemming</a> (<i>Dicrostonyx groenlandicus</i>), <a href="/wiki/Stoat" title="Stoat">stoat</a> (<i>Mustela erminea</i>), and <a href="/wiki/Snowshoe_hare" title="Snowshoe hare">snowshoe hare</a> (<i>Lepus americanus</i>), <a href="/wiki/Seasonal_polyphenism" class="mw-redirect" title="Seasonal polyphenism">seasonal colour change</a> between brown in summer and white in winter is driven largely by camouflage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-115" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some arboreal mammals, notably primates and marsupials, have shades of violet, green, or blue skin on parts of their bodies, indicating some distinct advantage in their largely <a href="/wiki/Arboreal" class="mw-redirect" title="Arboreal">arboreal</a> habitat due to <a href="/wiki/Convergent_evolution" title="Convergent evolution">convergent evolution</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prum2004&#95;112-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prum2004-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Aposematism" title="Aposematism">Aposematism</a>, warning off possible predators, is the most likely explanation of the black-and-white pelage of many mammals which are able to defend themselves, such as in the foul-smelling <a href="/wiki/Skunk" title="Skunk">skunk</a> and the powerful and aggressive <a href="/wiki/Honey_badger" title="Honey badger">honey badger</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-116" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Coat colour is sometimes <a href="/wiki/Sexual_dimorphism" title="Sexual dimorphism">sexually dimorphic</a>, as in <a href="/wiki/Sexual_dimorphism_in_non-human_primates#Pelage_color_and_markings" title="Sexual dimorphism in non-human primates">many primate species</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-117" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-117"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Differences in female and male coat colour may indicate nutrition and hormone levels, important in mate selection.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-eva.mpg.de&#95;118-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eva.mpg.de-118"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Coat colour may influence the ability to retain heat, depending on how much light is reflected. Mammals with a darker coloured coat can absorb more heat from solar radiation, and stay warmer, and some smaller mammals, such as <a href="/wiki/Vole" title="Vole">voles</a>, have darker fur in the winter. The white, pigmentless fur of arctic mammals, such as the polar bear, may reflect more solar radiation directly onto the skin.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-6" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 166–167">&#58;&#8202;166–167&#8202;</span></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-dawson2014&#95;109-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dawson2014-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The dazzling black-and-white striping of <a href="/wiki/Zebra" title="Zebra">zebras</a> appear to provide some protection from biting flies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Caro&#95;119-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Caro-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Reproductive_system">Reproductive system</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Mammalian_reproduction" title="Mammalian reproduction">Mammalian reproduction</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Goat_family.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Goat_family.jpg/250px-Goat_family.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Goat_family.jpg/500px-Goat_family.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1600" data-file-height="1067" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Goat" title="Goat">Goat</a> kids stay with their mother until they are weaned.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mammals reproduce by <a href="/wiki/Internal_fertilisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Internal fertilisation">internal fertilisation</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-naguib&#95;120-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-naguib-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and are solely <a href="/wiki/Gonochorism" title="Gonochorism">gonochoric</a> (having either male or female <a href="/wiki/Sex_organ" title="Sex organ">sex organs</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-121" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  Male mammals <a href="/wiki/Ejaculation" title="Ejaculation">ejaculate</a> <a href="/wiki/Semen" title="Semen">semen</a> during <a href="/wiki/Copulation_(zoology)" title="Copulation (zoology)">copulation</a> through a <a href="/wiki/Penis" title="Penis">penis</a>, which may be contained in a <a href="/wiki/Penile_sheath" title="Penile sheath">prepuce</a> when not erect. Male placentals also <a href="/wiki/Urinate" class="mw-redirect" title="Urinate">urinate</a> through a penis, and some placentals also have a penis bone (<a href="/wiki/Baculum" title="Baculum">baculum</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Lombardi1998&#95;122-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lombardi1998-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hyman1992&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hyman1992-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-naguib&#95;120-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-naguib-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Marsupials typically have forked penises,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Tyndale-BiscoeRenfree1987&#95;124-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Tyndale-BiscoeRenfree1987-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> while <a href="/wiki/Echidna" title="Echidna">echidnas</a> have four-headed penises with two functioning heads.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-125" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Depending on the species, penile <a href="/wiki/Erection" title="Erection">erection</a> is driven either by blood flow into vascular, spongy tissue or by muscular action.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Lombardi1998&#95;122-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Lombardi1998-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>  The <a href="/wiki/Testicles" class="mw-redirect" title="Testicles">testicles</a> of most mammals descend into the <a href="/wiki/Scrotum" title="Scrotum">scrotum</a> which is typically posterior to the penis but is often anterior in marsupials. Female mammals generally have an external <a href="/wiki/Vulva" title="Vulva">vulva</a> and <a href="/wiki/Clitoris" title="Clitoris">clitoris</a> and <a href="/wiki/Labia" title="Labia">labia</a>, two internal paired <a href="/wiki/Oviduct" title="Oviduct">oviducts</a>, one or two <a href="/wiki/Uteri" class="mw-redirect" title="Uteri">uteri</a>, one or two <a href="/wiki/Cervix" title="Cervix">cervices</a> and a <a href="/wiki/Vagina" title="Vagina">vagina</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-126" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-127" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Marsupials have two lateral vaginas and a medial vagina. The "vagina" of monotremes is better understood as a "urogenital sinus". The uterine systems of placentals can be duplex, with two uteri and cervices which open into the vagina, or bipartite, with two <a href="/wiki/Uterine_horn" class="mw-redirect" title="Uterine horn">uterine horns</a> with a single cervix connected to the vagina, or bicornuate, with two uterine horns connected distally but separated medially in a Y-shape, or simplex, with a single uterus.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-128" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-129" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-7" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 220–221, 247">&#58;&#8202;220–221,&#8202;247&#8202;</span></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Dendrolagus_matschiei_1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Dendrolagus_matschiei_1.jpg/250px-Dendrolagus_matschiei_1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="233" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Dendrolagus_matschiei_1.jpg/500px-Dendrolagus_matschiei_1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="800" data-file-height="746" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Matschie%27s_tree-kangaroo" title="Matschie&#39;s tree-kangaroo">Matschie's tree-kangaroo</a> with young in pouch</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ancestral condition for mammal reproduction is the birthing of relatively undeveloped young, either through direct <a href="/wiki/Vivipary" title="Vivipary">vivipary</a> or a short period as soft-shelled eggs. This is likely due to the fact that the torso could not expand due to the presence of <a href="/wiki/Epipubic_bones" class="mw-redirect" title="Epipubic bones">epipubic bones</a>. The oldest demonstration of this reproductive style is with <i><a href="/wiki/Kayentatherium" title="Kayentatherium">Kayentatherium</a></i>, which produced undeveloped <a href="/wiki/Perinate" title="Perinate">perinates</a>, but at much higher litter sizes than any modern mammal, 38 specimens.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hoffman&amp;Rowe&#95;130-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hoffman&amp;Rowe-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most modern mammals are <a href="/wiki/Viviparity" title="Viviparity">viviparous</a>, giving birth to live young. However, the five species of monotreme, the platypus and the four species of echidna, lay eggs. The monotremes have a <a href="/wiki/Sex-determination_system" title="Sex-determination system">sex-determination system</a> different from that of most other mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-131" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In particular, the <a href="/wiki/Sex_chromosome" title="Sex chromosome">sex chromosomes</a> of a platypus are more like those of a chicken than those of a therian mammal.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-132" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Viviparous mammals are in the subclass Theria; those living today are in the marsupial and placental infraclasses. Marsupials have a short <a href="/wiki/Gestation" title="Gestation">gestation</a> period, typically shorter than its <a href="/wiki/Estrous_cycle" title="Estrous cycle">estrous cycle</a> and generally giving birth to a number of undeveloped newborns that then undergo further development; in many species, this takes place within a pouch-like sac, the <a href="/wiki/Pouch_(marsupial)" title="Pouch (marsupial)">marsupium</a>, located in the front of the mother's <a href="/wiki/Abdomen" title="Abdomen">abdomen</a>. This is the <a href="/wiki/Symplesiomorphy" class="mw-redirect" title="Symplesiomorphy">plesiomorphic</a> condition among viviparous mammals; the presence of epipubic bones in all non-placentals prevents the expansion of the torso needed for full pregnancy.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-schulkin&#95;79-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-schulkin-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Even non-placental eutherians probably reproduced this way.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Epipubic&#95;bones&#95;in&#95;eutherian&#95;mammals&#95;40-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Epipubic_bones_in_eutherian_mammals-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The placentals give birth to relatively complete and developed young, usually after long gestation periods.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-133" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They get their name from the <a href="/wiki/Placenta" title="Placenta">placenta</a>, which connects the developing fetus to the uterine wall to allow nutrient uptake.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-134" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In placentals, the epipubic is either completely lost or converted into the baculum; allowing the torso to be able to expand and thus birth developed offspring.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Hoffman&amp;Rowe&#95;130-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Hoffman&amp;Rowe-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The great majority of mammals give birth multiple times during their lifespan, but a few species are <a href="/wiki/Semelparous" class="mw-redirect" title="Semelparous">semelparous</a>.<sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (September 2025)">citation needed</span></a></i>&#93;</sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Mammary_gland" title="Mammary gland">mammary glands</a> of mammals are specialised to produce milk, the primary source of nutrition for newborns. The monotremes branched early from other mammals and do not have the <a href="/wiki/Teat" title="Teat">teats</a> seen in most mammals, but they do have mammary glands. The young lick the milk from a mammary patch on the mother's belly.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-135" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Compared to placental mammals, the milk of marsupials changes greatly in both production rate and in nutrient composition, due to the underdeveloped young. In addition, the mammary glands have more autonomy allowing them to supply separate milks to young at different development stages.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-136" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Lactose" title="Lactose">Lactose</a> is the main sugar in placental milk while monotreme and marsupial milk is dominated by <a href="/wiki/Oligosaccharide" title="Oligosaccharide">oligosaccharides</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-137" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Weaning" title="Weaning">Weaning</a> is the process in which a mammal becomes less dependent on their mother's milk and more on solid food.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-138" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Endothermy">Endothermy</h3></div>
<p>Nearly all mammals are <a href="/wiki/Endothermy" class="mw-redirect" title="Endothermy">endothermic</a> ("warm-blooded"). Most mammals also have hair to help keep them warm. Like birds, mammals can forage or hunt in weather and climates too cold for <a href="/wiki/Ectotherm" title="Ectotherm">ectothermic</a> ("cold-blooded") reptiles and insects. Endothermy requires plenty of food energy, so mammals eat more food per unit of body weight than most reptiles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-139" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Small insectivorous mammals eat prodigious amounts for their size. A rare exception, the <a href="/wiki/Naked_mole-rat" title="Naked mole-rat">naked mole-rat</a> produces little metabolic heat, so it is considered an operational <a href="/wiki/Poikilotherm" title="Poikilotherm">poikilotherm</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-140" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Birds are also endothermic, so endothermy is not unique to mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-141" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Species_lifespan">Species lifespan</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Life_expectancy" title="Life expectancy">Life expectancy</a> and <a href="/wiki/Maximum_life_span" title="Maximum life span">Maximum life span</a></div>
<p>Among mammals, species maximum lifespan varies significantly from one year in the <a href="/wiki/Yellow-sided_opossum" title="Yellow-sided opossum">yellow-sided opossum</a><sup class="noprint Inline-Template Template-Fact" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed" title="Wikipedia:Citation needed"><span title="This claim needs references to reliable sources. (September 2025)">citation needed</span></a></i>&#93;</sup> to 211 years in the oldest <a href="/wiki/Bowhead_whale" title="Bowhead whale">bowhead whale</a> recorded.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid19896964&#95;142-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid19896964-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Although the underlying basis for these lifespan differences is still uncertain, numerous studies indicate that the ability to <a href="/wiki/DNA_repair" title="DNA repair">repair DNA damage</a> is an important determinant of mammalian lifespan. In a 1974 study by Hart and Setlow,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid4526202&#95;143-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid4526202-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> it was found that DNA excision repair capability increased systematically with species lifespan among seven mammalian species. Species lifespan was observed to be robustly correlated with the capacity to recognise DNA double-strand breaks as well as the level of the DNA repair protein <a href="/wiki/Ku80" title="Ku80">Ku80</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid19896964&#95;142-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid19896964-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In a study of the cells from sixteen mammalian species, genes employed in DNA repair were found to be <a href="/wiki/Downregulation_and_upregulation" title="Downregulation and upregulation">up-regulated</a> in the longer-lived species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid27874830&#95;144-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid27874830-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The cellular level of the DNA repair enzyme <a href="/wiki/Poly_ADP_ribose_polymerase" class="mw-redirect" title="Poly ADP ribose polymerase">poly ADP ribose polymerase</a> was found to correlate with species lifespan in a study of 13 mammalian species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid1465394&#95;145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid1465394-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Three additional studies of a variety of mammalian species also reported a correlation between species lifespan and DNA repair capability.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid7266079&#95;146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid7266079-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid7060140&#95;147-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid7060140-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-pmid3974310&#95;148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pmid3974310-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Locomotion">Locomotion</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Animal_locomotion" title="Animal locomotion">Animal locomotion</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Terrestrial">Terrestrial</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Terrestrial_locomotion" title="Terrestrial locomotion">Terrestrial locomotion</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Muybridge_race_horse_animated.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Muybridge_race_horse_animated.gif/250px-Muybridge_race_horse_animated.gif" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/dd/Muybridge_race_horse_animated.gif 2x" data-file-width="300" data-file-height="200" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Running" title="Running">Running gait</a>. Photographs by <a href="/wiki/Eadweard_Muybridge" title="Eadweard Muybridge">Eadweard Muybridge</a>, 1887</figcaption></figure>
<p>Most vertebrates are <a href="/wiki/Plantigrade" title="Plantigrade">plantigrade</a>, walking on the whole of the underside of the foot. Many mammals, such as cats and dogs, are <a href="/wiki/Digitigrade" title="Digitigrade">digitigrade</a>, walking on their toes, the greater stride length allowing more speed. Some animals such as <a href="/wiki/Horse" title="Horse">horses</a> are <a href="/wiki/Unguligrade" class="mw-redirect" title="Unguligrade">unguligrade</a>, walking on the tips of their toes. This even further increases their stride length and thus their speed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-149" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A few mammals, namely the great apes, are also known to <a href="/wiki/Knuckle-walking" title="Knuckle-walking">walk on their knuckles</a>, at least for their front legs. <a href="/wiki/Giant_anteater" title="Giant anteater">Giant anteaters</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-150" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and platypuses<sup id="cite&#95;ref-151" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> are also knuckle-walkers. Some mammals are <a href="/wiki/Bipedalism" title="Bipedalism">bipeds</a>, using only two limbs for locomotion, which can be seen in, for example, humans and the great apes. Bipedal species have a larger field of <a href="/wiki/Mammalian_vision" title="Mammalian vision">vision</a> than quadrupeds, conserve more energy and have the ability to manipulate objects with their hands, which aids in foraging. Instead of walking, some bipeds hop, such as kangaroos and <a href="/wiki/Kangaroo_rat" title="Kangaroo rat">kangaroo rats</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-152" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-153" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Animals will use different gaits for different speeds, terrain and situations. For example, horses show four natural gaits, the slowest <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait" title="Horse gait">horse gait</a> is the <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait#Walk" title="Horse gait">walk</a>, then there are three faster gaits which, from slowest to fastest, are the <a href="/wiki/Trot_(horse_gait)" class="mw-redirect" title="Trot (horse gait)">trot</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Canter" class="mw-redirect" title="Canter">canter</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Horse_gait#Gallop" title="Horse gait">gallop</a>. Animals may also have unusual gaits that are used occasionally, such as for moving sideways or backwards. For example, the main <a href="/wiki/Gait_(human)" title="Gait (human)">human gaits</a> are bipedal <a href="/wiki/Walking" title="Walking">walking</a> and <a href="/wiki/Running" title="Running">running</a>, but they employ many other gaits occasionally, including a four-legged <a href="/wiki/Crawling_(human)" title="Crawling (human)">crawl</a> in tight spaces.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-dagg&#95;154-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dagg-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals show a vast range of <a href="/wiki/Gait" title="Gait">gaits</a>, the order that they place and lift their appendages in locomotion. Gaits can be grouped into categories according to their patterns of support sequence. For quadrupeds, there are three main categories: walking gaits, running gaits and <a href="/wiki/Leaping_gaits" class="mw-redirect" title="Leaping gaits">leaping gaits</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-155" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Walking is the most common gait, where some feet are on the ground at any given time, and found in almost all legged animals. Running is considered to occur when at some points in the stride all feet are off the ground in a moment of suspension.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-dagg&#95;154-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dagg-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Arboreal">Arboreal</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Arboreal_locomotion" title="Arboreal locomotion">Arboreal locomotion</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Brachiating_Gibbon_(Some_rights_reserved).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Brachiating_Gibbon_%28Some_rights_reserved%29.jpg/250px-Brachiating_Gibbon_%28Some_rights_reserved%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="285" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/d2/Brachiating_Gibbon_%28Some_rights_reserved%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="333" data-file-height="500" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Gibbon" title="Gibbon">Gibbons</a> are very good <a href="/wiki/Brachiation" title="Brachiation">brachiators</a> because their elongated limbs enable them to easily swing and grasp on to branches.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Arboreal animals frequently have elongated limbs that help them cross gaps, reach fruit or other resources, test the firmness of support ahead and, in some cases, to <a href="/wiki/Brachiation" title="Brachiation">brachiate</a> (swing between trees).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Cartmill&#95;156-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cartmill-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many arboreal species, such as tree porcupines, <a href="/wiki/Silky_anteater" title="Silky anteater">silky anteaters</a>, spider monkeys, and <a href="/wiki/Phalangeriformes" title="Phalangeriformes">possums</a>, use <a href="/wiki/Prehensile_tail" title="Prehensile tail">prehensile tails</a> to grasp branches. In the spider monkey, the tip of the tail has either a bare patch or adhesive pad, which provides increased friction. Claws can be used to interact with rough substrates and reorient the direction of forces the animal applies. This is what allows <a href="/wiki/Squirrel" title="Squirrel">squirrels</a> to climb tree trunks that are so large to be essentially flat from the perspective of such a small animal. However, claws can interfere with an animal's ability to grasp very small branches, as they may wrap too far around and prick the animal's own paw. Frictional gripping is used by primates, relying upon hairless fingertips. Squeezing the branch between the fingertips generates frictional force that holds the animal's hand to the branch. However, this type of grip depends upon the angle of the frictional force, thus upon the diameter of the branch, with larger branches resulting in reduced gripping ability. To control descent, especially down large diameter branches, some arboreal animals such as squirrels have evolved highly mobile ankle joints that permit rotating the foot into a 'reversed' posture. This allows the claws to hook into the rough surface of the bark, opposing the force of gravity. Small size provides many advantages to arboreal species: such as increasing the relative size of branches to the animal, lower centre of mass, increased stability, lower mass (allowing movement on smaller branches) and the ability to move through more cluttered habitat.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Cartmill&#95;156-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cartmill-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Size relating to weight affects gliding animals such as the <a href="/wiki/Sugar_glider" title="Sugar glider">sugar glider</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-157" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some species of primate, bat and all species of <a href="/wiki/Sloth" title="Sloth">sloth</a> achieve passive stability by hanging beneath the branch. Both pitching and tipping become irrelevant, as the only method of failure would be losing their grip.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Cartmill&#95;156-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cartmill-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Aerial">Aerial</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Aerial_locomotion" class="mw-redirect" title="Aerial locomotion">Aerial locomotion</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_1" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm/500px--Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="340" height="191" data-durationhint="102" data-mwtitle="Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp8&quot;" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/5/52/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm/Israeli_Bats_-_26_September_2015.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Slow-motion and normal speed of <a href="/wiki/Egyptian_fruit_bat" title="Egyptian fruit bat">Egyptian fruit bats</a> flying</figcaption></figure>
<p>Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly. They fly through the air at a constant speed by moving their wings up and down (usually with some fore-aft movement as well). Because the animal is in motion, there is some airflow relative to its body which, combined with the velocity of the wings, generates a faster airflow moving over the wing. This generates a lift force vector pointing forwards and upwards, and a drag force vector pointing rearwards and upwards. The upwards components of these counteract gravity, keeping the body in the air, while the forward component provides thrust to counteract both the drag from the wing and from the body as a whole.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-158" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The wings of bats are much thinner and consist of more bones than those of birds, allowing bats to manoeuvre more accurately and fly with more lift and less drag.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-159" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-anders&#95;160-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-anders-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> By folding the wings inwards towards their body on the upstroke, they use 35% less energy during flight than birds.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-161" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The membranes are delicate, ripping easily; however, the tissue of the bat's membrane is able to regrow, such that small tears can heal quickly.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-162" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The surface of their wings is equipped with touch-sensitive receptors on small bumps called <a href="/wiki/Merkel_cell" title="Merkel cell">Merkel cells</a>, also found on human fingertips. These sensitive areas are different in bats, as each bump has a tiny hair in the centre, making it even more sensitive and allowing the bat to detect and collect information about the air flowing over its wings, and to fly more efficiently by changing the shape of its wings in response.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-163" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Fossorial_and_subterranean">Fossorial and subterranean</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Fossorial" title="Fossorial">Fossorial</a> and <a href="/wiki/Burrow" title="Burrow">Burrow</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:187px;max-width:187px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:138px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Wombat3.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Wombat3.jpg/250px-Wombat3.jpg" decoding="async" width="185" height="139" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Wombat3.jpg/500px-Wombat3.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1600" data-file-height="1200" /></a></span></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:201px;max-width:201px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:138px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ScalopusAquaticus.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/ScalopusAquaticus.jpg/250px-ScalopusAquaticus.jpg" decoding="async" width="199" height="138" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/ScalopusAquaticus.jpg/500px-ScalopusAquaticus.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1500" data-file-height="1042" /></a></span></div></div></div><div class="trow" style="display:flex"><div class="thumbcaption">Semi-fossorial <a href="/wiki/Southern_hairy-nosed_wombat" title="Southern hairy-nosed wombat">wombat</a> (left) vs. fully fossorial <a href="/wiki/Eastern_mole" title="Eastern mole">eastern mole</a> (right)</div></div></div></div>
<p>A fossorial (from Latin <i>fossor</i>, meaning "digger") is an animal adapted to digging which lives primarily, but not solely, underground. Some examples are <a href="/wiki/Badger" title="Badger">badgers</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Naked_mole-rat" title="Naked mole-rat">naked mole-rats</a>. Many <a href="/wiki/Rodent" title="Rodent">rodent</a> species are also considered fossorial because they live in burrows for most but not all of the day. Species that live exclusively underground are subterranean, and those with limited adaptations to a fossorial lifestyle sub-fossorial. Some organisms are fossorial to aid in <a href="/wiki/Temperature_regulation" class="mw-redirect" title="Temperature regulation">temperature regulation</a> while others use the underground habitat for protection from <a href="/wiki/Predator" class="mw-redirect" title="Predator">predators</a> or for <a href="/wiki/Food_storage" title="Food storage">food storage</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;164-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Fossorial mammals have a fusiform body, thickest at the shoulders and tapering off at the tail and nose. Unable to see in the dark burrows, most have degenerated eyes, but degeneration varies between species; <a href="/wiki/Pocket_gopher" class="mw-redirect" title="Pocket gopher">pocket gophers</a>, for example, are only semi-fossorial and have very small yet functional eyes, in the fully fossorial <a href="/wiki/Marsupial_mole" title="Marsupial mole">marsupial mole</a>, the eyes are degenerated and useless, <i><a href="/wiki/Talpa_(genus)" class="mw-redirect" title="Talpa (genus)">Talpa</a></i> moles have <a href="/wiki/Vestigial" class="mw-redirect" title="Vestigial">vestigial</a> eyes and the <a href="/wiki/Cape_golden_mole" title="Cape golden mole">Cape golden mole</a> has a layer of skin covering the eyes. External ears flaps are also very small or absent. Truly fossorial mammals have short, stout legs as strength is more important than speed to a burrowing mammal, but semi-fossorial mammals have <a href="/wiki/Cursorial" title="Cursorial">cursorial</a> legs. The front paws are broad and have strong claws to help in loosening dirt while excavating burrows, and the back paws have webbing, as well as claws, which aids in throwing loosened dirt backwards. Most have large incisors to prevent dirt from flying into their mouth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-165" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many fossorial mammals such as shrews, hedgehogs, and moles were classified under the now obsolete order <a href="/wiki/Insectivora" title="Insectivora">Insectivora</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-166" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Aquatic">Aquatic</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Aquatic_locomotion" title="Aquatic locomotion">Aquatic locomotion</a>, <a href="/wiki/Marine_mammal" title="Marine mammal">Marine mammal</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Aquatic_mammal" title="Aquatic mammal">Aquatic mammal</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_2" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv/250px--Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="167" data-durationhint="45" data-mwtitle="Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a0/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="720" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a0/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv" type="video/ogg;
