codecs=&quot;theora, vorbis&quot;" data-width="720" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a0/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="216" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a0/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv/Living-on-the-Edge-Settlement-Patterns-by-the-Symbiotic-Barnacle-Xenobalanus-globicipitis-on-Small-pone.0127367.s001.ogv.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="360" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>A pod of <a href="/wiki/Short-beaked_common_dolphin" class="mw-redirect" title="Short-beaked common dolphin">short-beaked common dolphins</a> swimming</figcaption></figure>
<p>Fully aquatic mammals, the cetaceans and <a href="/wiki/Sirenia" title="Sirenia">sirenians</a>, have lost their legs and have a tail fin to propel themselves through the water. <a href="/wiki/Flipper_(anatomy)" title="Flipper (anatomy)">Flipper</a> movement is continuous. Whales swim by moving their tail fin and lower body up and down, propelling themselves through vertical movement, while their flippers are mainly used for steering. Their skeletal anatomy allows them to be fast swimmers. Most species have a <a href="/wiki/Dorsal_fin" title="Dorsal fin">dorsal fin</a> to prevent themselves from turning upside-down in the water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-167" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-168" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The flukes of sirenians are raised up and down in long strokes to move the animal forward, and can be twisted to turn. The forelimbs are paddle-like flippers which aid in turning and slowing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-169" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Semi-aquatic" class="mw-redirect" title="Semi-aquatic">Semi-aquatic</a> mammals, like pinnipeds, have two pairs of flippers on the front and back, the fore-flippers and hind-flippers. The elbows and ankles are enclosed within the body.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Berta63&#95;170-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Berta63-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fish&#95;2003&#95;171-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fish_2003-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Pinnipeds have several adaptions for reducing <a href="/wiki/Drag_(physics)" title="Drag (physics)">drag</a>. In addition to their streamlined bodies, they have smooth networks of <a href="/wiki/Muscle_fascicle" title="Muscle fascicle">muscle bundles</a> in their skin that may increase <a href="/wiki/Laminar_flow" title="Laminar flow">laminar flow</a> and make it easier for them to slip through water. They also lack <a href="/wiki/Arrector_pili_muscle" title="Arrector pili muscle">arrector pili</a>, so their fur can be streamlined as they swim.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Riedman3&#95;172-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Riedman3-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They rely on their fore-flippers for locomotion in a wing-like manner similar to <a href="/wiki/Penguin" title="Penguin">penguins</a> and <a href="/wiki/Sea_turtles" class="mw-redirect" title="Sea turtles">sea turtles</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fish1996&#95;173-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fish1996-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Fore-flipper movement is not continuous, and the animal glides between each stroke.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Fish&#95;2003&#95;171-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Fish_2003-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Compared to terrestrial carnivorans, the fore-limbs are reduced in length, which gives the locomotor muscles at the shoulder and elbow joints greater mechanical advantage;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Berta63&#95;170-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Berta63-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the hind-flippers serve as stabilizers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Riedman3&#95;172-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Riedman3-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other semi-aquatic mammals include beavers, <a href="/wiki/Hippopotamus" title="Hippopotamus">hippopotamuses</a>, <a href="/wiki/Otter" title="Otter">otters</a> and platypuses.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-174" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Hippos are very large semi-aquatic mammals, and their barrel-shaped bodies have <a href="https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/graviportal" class="extiw" title="wikt:graviportal">graviportal</a> skeletal structures,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-175" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> adapted to carrying their enormous weight, and their <a href="/wiki/Specific_gravity" class="mw-redirect" title="Specific gravity">specific gravity</a> allows them to sink and move along the bottom of a river.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-176" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Behavior">Behavior</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Communication_and_vocalisation">Communication and vocalisation</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Animal_communication" title="Animal communication">Animal communication</a> and <a href="/wiki/Animal_echolocation" title="Animal echolocation">Animal echolocation</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Monkey_%26_Baby.JPG" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Monkey_%26_Baby.JPG/250px-Monkey_%26_Baby.JPG" decoding="async" width="190" height="198" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Monkey_%26_Baby.JPG/500px-Monkey_%26_Baby.JPG 2x" data-file-width="2348" data-file-height="2448" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Vervet_monkey" title="Vervet monkey">Vervet monkeys</a> use at least four distinct <a href="/wiki/Alarm_signal" title="Alarm signal">alarm calls</a> for different <a href="/wiki/Predator" class="mw-redirect" title="Predator">predators</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Seyfarth&#95;177-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Seyfarth-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Many mammals communicate by vocalising. Vocal communication serves many purposes, including in mating rituals, as <a href="/wiki/Alarm_signal" title="Alarm signal">warning calls</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-178" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> to indicate food sources, and for social purposes. Males often call during mating rituals to ward off other males and to attract females, as in the <a href="/wiki/Roar_(vocalization)" class="mw-redirect" title="Roar (vocalization)">roaring</a> of <a href="/wiki/Lion" title="Lion">lions</a> and <a href="/wiki/Red_deer" title="Red deer">red deer</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-179" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Whale_song" class="mw-redirect" title="Whale song">songs</a> of the humpback whale may be signals to females;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-180" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> they have different dialects in different regions of the ocean.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-181" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Social vocalisations include the <a href="/wiki/Territory_(animal)" title="Territory (animal)">territorial</a> calls of <a href="/wiki/Gibbon" title="Gibbon">gibbons</a>, and the use of frequency in <a href="/wiki/Greater_spear-nosed_bat" title="Greater spear-nosed bat">greater spear-nosed bats</a> to distinguish between groups.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-182" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Vervet_monkey" title="Vervet monkey">vervet monkey</a> gives a distinct alarm call for each of at least four different predators, and the reactions of other monkeys vary according to the call. For example, if an alarm call signals a python, the monkeys climb into the trees, whereas the eagle alarm causes monkeys to seek a hiding place on the ground.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Seyfarth&#95;177-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Seyfarth-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Prairie_dogs" class="mw-redirect" title="Prairie dogs">Prairie dogs</a> similarly have complex calls that signal the type, size, and speed of an approaching predator.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-183" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Elephants communicate socially with a variety of sounds including snorting, screaming, trumpeting, roaring and rumbling. Some of the rumbling calls are <a href="/wiki/Infrasound" title="Infrasound">infrasonic</a>, below the hearing range of humans, and can be heard by other elephants up to 6 miles (9.7&#160;km) away at still times near sunrise and sunset.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-184" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><audio id="mwe_player_3" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" style="width:250px;" data-durationhint="30" data-mwtitle="Killer_whale.ogg" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Killer_whale.ogg"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/7/79/Killer_whale.ogg" type="audio/ogg; codecs=&quot;vorbis&quot;" data-width="0" data-height="0" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/7/79/Killer_whale.ogg/Killer_whale.ogg.mp3" type="audio/mpeg" data-transcodekey="mp3" data-width="0" data-height="0" /></audio></span><figcaption>Orca calling including occasional echolocation clicks</figcaption></figure>
<p>Mammals signal by a variety of means. Many give visual <a href="/wiki/Anti-predator_adaptation" title="Anti-predator adaptation">anti-predator signals</a>, as when deer and <a href="/wiki/Gazelle" title="Gazelle">gazelle</a> <a href="/wiki/Stotting" title="Stotting">stot</a>, <a href="/wiki/Honest_signal" class="mw-redirect" title="Honest signal">honestly indicating</a> their fit condition and their ability to escape,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-185" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-186" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or when <a href="/wiki/White-tailed_deer" title="White-tailed deer">white-tailed deer</a> and other prey mammals flag with conspicuous tail markings when alarmed, informing the predator that it has been detected.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-187" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many mammals make use of <a href="/wiki/Scent-marking" class="mw-redirect" title="Scent-marking">scent-marking</a>, sometimes possibly to help defend territory, but probably with a range of functions both within and between species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-188" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-189" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Microbat" title="Microbat">Microbats</a> and <a href="/wiki/Toothed_whale" title="Toothed whale">toothed whales</a> including <a href="/wiki/Oceanic_dolphin" title="Oceanic dolphin">oceanic dolphins</a> vocalise both socially and in <a href="/wiki/Animal_echolocation" title="Animal echolocation">echolocation</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Feeding">Feeding</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_4" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm/250px--Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="58" data-mwtitle="Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a6/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a6/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9&quot;" data-width="1280" data-height="720" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a6/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/a6/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm/Short-beaked_echidna_in_suburban-Sydney_backyard.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Short-beaked_echidna" title="Short-beaked echidna">short-beaked echidna</a> foraging for insects</figcaption></figure>
<p>To maintain a high constant body temperature is energy expensive—mammals therefore need a nutritious and plentiful diet. While the earliest mammals were probably predators, different species have since adapted to meet their dietary requirements in a variety of ways. Some eat other animals—this is a <a href="/wiki/Carnivore" title="Carnivore">carnivorous</a> diet (and includes insectivorous diets). Other mammals, called <a href="/wiki/Herbivore" title="Herbivore">herbivores</a>, eat plants, which contain <a href="/wiki/Complex_carbohydrate" class="mw-redirect" title="Complex carbohydrate">complex carbohydrates</a> such as cellulose. An herbivorous diet includes subtypes such as <a href="/wiki/Granivory" class="mw-redirect" title="Granivory">granivory</a> (seed eating), <a href="/wiki/Folivory" class="mw-redirect" title="Folivory">folivory</a> (leaf eating), <a href="/wiki/Frugivory" class="mw-redirect" title="Frugivory">frugivory</a> (fruit eating), <a href="/wiki/Nectarivory" class="mw-redirect" title="Nectarivory">nectarivory</a> (nectar eating), <a href="/wiki/Gummivory" class="mw-redirect" title="Gummivory">gummivory</a> (gum eating) and <a href="/wiki/Mycophagy" class="mw-redirect" title="Mycophagy">mycophagy</a> (fungus eating). The digestive tract of an herbivore is host to bacteria that ferment these complex substances, and make them available for digestion, which are either housed in the multichambered <a href="/wiki/Stomach" title="Stomach">stomach</a> or in a large cecum.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Comparative&#95;anatomy&#95;of&#95;the&#95;stomach&#95;93-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Comparative_anatomy_of_the_stomach-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some mammals are <a href="/wiki/Coprophagous" class="mw-redirect" title="Coprophagous">coprophagous</a>, consuming <a href="/wiki/Feces" title="Feces">feces</a> to absorb the nutrients not digested when the food was first ingested.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hair&#95;89-8" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hair-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 131–137">&#58;&#8202;131–137&#8202;</span></sup> An <a href="/wiki/Omnivore" title="Omnivore">omnivore</a> eats both prey and plants. Carnivorous mammals have a simple <a href="/wiki/Digestive_system" class="mw-redirect" title="Digestive system">digestive tract</a> because the <a href="/wiki/Protein" title="Protein">proteins</a>, <a href="/wiki/Lipid" title="Lipid">lipids</a> and <a href="/wiki/Mineral" title="Mineral">minerals</a> found in meat require little in the way of specialised digestion. Exceptions to this include <a href="/wiki/Baleen_whale" title="Baleen whale">baleen whales</a> who also house <a href="/wiki/Gut_flora" class="mw-redirect" title="Gut flora">gut flora</a> in a multi-chambered stomach, like terrestrial herbivores.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-194" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The size of an animal is also a factor in determining diet type (<a href="/wiki/Allen%27s_rule" title="Allen&#39;s rule">Allen's rule</a>). Since small mammals have a high ratio of heat-losing surface area to heat-generating volume, they tend to have high energy requirements and a high <a href="/wiki/Metabolism" title="Metabolism">metabolic rate</a>. Mammals that weigh less than about 18 ounces (510&#160;g; 1.1&#160;lb) are mostly insectivorous because they cannot tolerate the slow, complex digestive process of an herbivore. Larger animals, on the other hand, generate more heat and less of this heat is lost. They can therefore tolerate either a slower collection process (carnivores that feed on larger vertebrates) or a slower digestive process (herbivores).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-195" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Furthermore, mammals that weigh more than 18 ounces (510&#160;g; 1.1&#160;lb) usually cannot collect enough insects during their waking hours to sustain themselves. The only large insectivorous mammals are those that feed on huge colonies of insects (<a href="/wiki/Ant" title="Ant">ants</a> or <a href="/wiki/Termite" title="Termite">termites</a>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Smithsonian&#95;Animal&#95;196-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Smithsonian_Animal-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:408px;max-width:408px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:202px;max-width:202px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Blacky.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Blacky.jpg/250px-Blacky.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="150" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Blacky.jpg/500px-Blacky.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2016" data-file-height="1512" /></a></span></div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:202px;max-width:202px"><div class="thumbimage"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:A_polar_bear_(Ursus_maritimus)_scavenging_a_narwhal_whale_(Monodon_monoceros)_carcass_-_journal.pone.0060797.g001-A.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/A_polar_bear_%28Ursus_maritimus%29_scavenging_a_narwhal_whale_%28Monodon_monoceros%29_carcass_-_journal.pone.0060797.g001-A.png/250px-A_polar_bear_%28Ursus_maritimus%29_scavenging_a_narwhal_whale_%28Monodon_monoceros%29_carcass_-_journal.pone.0060797.g001-A.png" decoding="async" width="200" height="99" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/A_polar_bear_%28Ursus_maritimus%29_scavenging_a_narwhal_whale_%28Monodon_monoceros%29_carcass_-_journal.pone.0060797.g001-A.png/500px-A_polar_bear_%28Ursus_maritimus%29_scavenging_a_narwhal_whale_%28Monodon_monoceros%29_carcass_-_journal.pone.0060797.g001-A.png 2x" data-file-width="1170" data-file-height="578" /></a></span></div></div></div><div class="trow" style="display:flex"><div class="thumbcaption">The <a href="/wiki/Hypocarnivore" title="Hypocarnivore">hypocarnivorous</a> <a href="/wiki/American_black_bear" title="American black bear">American black bear</a> (<i>Ursus americanus</i>) vs. the <a href="/wiki/Hypercarnivorous" class="mw-redirect" title="Hypercarnivorous">hypercarnivorous</a> <a href="/wiki/Polar_bear" title="Polar bear">polar bear</a> (<i>Ursus maritimus</i>)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-valkenburgh2007&#95;197-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-valkenburgh2007-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></div></div></div></div>
<p>Some mammals are omnivores and display varying degrees of carnivory and herbivory, generally leaning in favour of one more than the other. Since plants and meat are digested differently, there is a preference for one over the other, as in bears where some species may be mostly carnivorous and others mostly herbivorous.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-198" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They are grouped into three categories: <a href="/wiki/Mesocarnivore" title="Mesocarnivore">mesocarnivory</a> (50–70% meat), <a href="/wiki/Hypercarnivore" title="Hypercarnivore">hypercarnivory</a> (70% and greater of meat), and <a href="/wiki/Hypocarnivore" title="Hypocarnivore">hypocarnivory</a> (50% or less of meat). The dentition of hypocarnivores consists of dull, triangular carnassial teeth meant for grinding food. Hypercarnivores, however, have conical teeth and sharp carnassials meant for slashing, and in some cases strong jaws for bone-crushing, as in the case of <a href="/wiki/Hyena" title="Hyena">hyenas</a>, allowing them to consume bones; some extinct groups, notably the <a href="/wiki/Machairodontinae" title="Machairodontinae">Machairodontinae</a>, had sabre-shaped <a href="/wiki/Maxillary_canine" title="Maxillary canine">canines</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-valkenburgh2007&#95;197-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-valkenburgh2007-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some physiological carnivores consume plant matter and some physiological herbivores consume meat. From a behavioural aspect, this would make them omnivores, but from the physiological standpoint, this may be due to <a href="/wiki/Zoopharmacognosy" title="Zoopharmacognosy">zoopharmacognosy</a>. Physiologically, animals must be able to obtain both energy and nutrients from plant and animal materials to be considered omnivorous. Thus, such animals are still able to be classified as carnivores and herbivores when they are just obtaining nutrients from materials originating from sources that do not seemingly complement their classification.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-199" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For example, it is well documented that some ungulates such as giraffes, camels, and cattle, will gnaw on bones to consume particular minerals and nutrients.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-200" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-200"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Also, cats, which are generally regarded as obligate carnivores, occasionally eat grass to regurgitate indigestible material (such as <a href="/wiki/Hairball" title="Hairball">hairballs</a>), aid with haemoglobin production, and as a laxative.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-201" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many mammals, in the absence of sufficient food requirements in an environment, suppress their metabolism and conserve energy in a process known as <a href="/wiki/Hibernation" title="Hibernation">hibernation</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-202" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the period preceding hibernation, larger mammals, such as bears, become <a href="/wiki/Polyphagic" class="mw-redirect" title="Polyphagic">polyphagic</a> to increase fat stores, whereas smaller mammals prefer to collect and stash food.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-203" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The slowing of the metabolism is accompanied by a decreased heart and respiratory rate, as well as a drop in internal temperatures, which can be around ambient temperature in some cases. For example, the internal temperatures of hibernating <a href="/wiki/Arctic_ground_squirrel" title="Arctic ground squirrel">Arctic ground squirrels</a> can drop to −2.9&#160;°C (26.8&#160;°F); however, the head and neck always stay above 0&#160;°C (32&#160;°F).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-204" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A few mammals in hot environments <a href="/wiki/Aestivate" class="mw-redirect" title="Aestivate">aestivate</a> in times of drought or extreme heat, for example the <a href="/wiki/Fat-tailed_dwarf_lemur" title="Fat-tailed dwarf lemur">fat-tailed dwarf lemur</a> (<i>Cheirogaleus medius</i>).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-205" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Drinking">Drinking</h3></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1066933788">.mw-parser-output .excerpt-hat .mw-editsection-like{font-style:normal}</style><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/Drinking#In_other_land_mammals" title="Drinking">Drinking § In other land mammals</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Drinking&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_5" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm/250px--Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="24" data-mwtitle="Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ac/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ac/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9&quot;" data-width="1280" data-height="720" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ac/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ac/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm/Cat_lapping_water_off_ground_in_slow_motion.gk.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Cat lapping water in slow motion</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Jack_Russell_Terrier_takes_a_drink.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Jack_Russell_Terrier_takes_a_drink.jpg/250px-Jack_Russell_Terrier_takes_a_drink.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="290" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Jack_Russell_Terrier_takes_a_drink.jpg/500px-Jack_Russell_Terrier_takes_a_drink.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1050" data-file-height="1604" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Jack_Russell_Terrier" title="Jack Russell Terrier">Jack Russell Terrier</a> laps in water with its tongue. </figcaption></figure>
<p>By necessity, <a href="/wiki/Terrestrial_animal" title="Terrestrial animal">terrestrial animals</a> in captivity become accustomed to drinking water, but most free-roaming animals stay hydrated through the fluids and moisture in fresh food,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Mayer59&#95;206-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Mayer59-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and learn to actively seek foods with high fluid content.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;pmid35831501&#95;207-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_pmid35831501-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> When conditions impel them to drink from bodies of water, the methods and motions differ greatly among species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Broom105&#95;208-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Broom105-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Cats" class="mw-redirect" title="Cats">Cats</a>, <a href="/wiki/Canidae" title="Canidae">canines</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Ruminants" class="mw-redirect" title="Ruminants">ruminants</a> all lower the neck and lap in water with their powerful tongues.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Broom105&#95;208-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Broom105-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Cats and canines lap up water with the tongue in a spoon-like shape.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Smith238&#95;209-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Smith238-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Canines lap water by scooping it into their mouth with a tongue which has taken the shape of a ladle. However, with cats, only the tip of their tongue (which is smooth) touches the water, and then the cat quickly pulls its tongue back into its mouth which soon closes; this results in a column of liquid being pulled into the cat's mouth, which is then secured by its mouth closing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-210" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Ruminants and most other herbivores partially submerge the tip of the mouth in order to draw in water by means of a plunging action with the tongue held straight.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Smith237&#95;211-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Smith237-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Cats drink at a significantly slower pace than ruminants, who face greater natural predation hazards.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Broom105&#95;208-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Broom105-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Many <a href="/wiki/Desert_animals" class="mw-redirect" title="Desert animals">desert animals</a> do not drink even if water becomes available, but rely on eating <a href="/wiki/Succulent_plant" title="Succulent plant">succulent plants</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Broom105&#95;208-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Broom105-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In cold and frozen environments, some animals like <a href="/wiki/Hares" class="mw-redirect" title="Hares">hares</a>, <a href="/wiki/Tree_squirrels" class="mw-redirect" title="Tree squirrels">tree squirrels</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Bighorn_sheep" title="Bighorn sheep">bighorn sheep</a> resort to consuming snow and icicles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-212" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In <a href="/wiki/Savannas" class="mw-redirect" title="Savannas">savannas</a>, the drinking method of <a href="/wiki/Giraffes" class="mw-redirect" title="Giraffes">giraffes</a> has been a source of speculation for its apparent defiance of gravity; the most recent theory contemplates the animal's long neck functions like a <a href="/wiki/Plunger_pump" title="Plunger pump">plunger pump</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-213" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Uniquely, <a href="/wiki/Elephants" class="mw-redirect" title="Elephants">elephants</a> draw water into their trunks and squirt it into their mouths.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Drinking&#95;Broom105&#95;208-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Drinking_Broom105-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
</div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Intelligence">Intelligence</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Animal_cognition" title="Animal cognition">Animal cognition</a></div>
<p>In intelligent mammals, such as <a href="/wiki/Primate" title="Primate">primates</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Cerebrum" title="Cerebrum">cerebrum</a> is larger relative to the rest of the brain. <a href="/wiki/Intelligence" title="Intelligence">Intelligence</a> itself is not easy to define, but indications of intelligence include the ability to learn, matched with behavioural flexibility. <a href="/wiki/Rat_IQ" class="mw-redirect" title="Rat IQ">Rats</a>, for example, are considered to be highly intelligent, as they can learn and perform new tasks, an ability that may be important when they first colonise a fresh <a href="/wiki/Biome" title="Biome">habitat</a>. In some mammals, food gathering appears to be related to intelligence: a deer feeding on plants has a brain smaller than a cat, which must think to outwit its prey.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Smithsonian&#95;Animal&#95;196-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Smithsonian_Animal-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:A_Bonobo_at_the_San_Diego_Zoo_%22fishing%22_for_termites.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/A_Bonobo_at_the_San_Diego_Zoo_%22fishing%22_for_termites.jpg/250px-A_Bonobo_at_the_San_Diego_Zoo_%22fishing%22_for_termites.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/A_Bonobo_at_the_San_Diego_Zoo_%22fishing%22_for_termites.jpg/500px-A_Bonobo_at_the_San_Diego_Zoo_%22fishing%22_for_termites.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2608" data-file-height="1952" /></a><figcaption>A <a href="/wiki/Bonobo" title="Bonobo">bonobo</a> fishing for <a href="/wiki/Termite" title="Termite">termites</a> with a stick</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Tool_use_by_non-humans" title="Tool use by non-humans">Tool use by animals</a> may indicate different levels of <a href="/wiki/Learning" title="Learning">learning</a> and <a href="/wiki/Animal_cognition" title="Animal cognition">cognition</a>. The <a href="/wiki/Tool_use_by_sea_otters" title="Tool use by sea otters">sea otter</a> uses rocks as essential and regular parts of its foraging behaviour (smashing <a href="/wiki/Abalone" title="Abalone">abalone</a> from rocks or breaking open shells), with some populations spending 21% of their time making tools.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-214" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other tool use, such as <a href="/wiki/Chimpanzee" title="Chimpanzee">chimpanzees</a> using twigs to "fish" for termites, may be developed by <a href="/wiki/Observational_learning" title="Observational learning">watching others use tools</a> and may even be a true example of animal teaching.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-215" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Tools may even be used in solving puzzles in which the animal appears to experience a <a href="/wiki/Eureka_effect" title="Eureka effect">"Eureka moment"</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-216" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other mammals that do not use tools, such as dogs, can also experience a Eureka moment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-217" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Brain_size" title="Brain size">Brain size</a> was previously considered a major indicator of the intelligence of an animal. Since most of the brain is used for maintaining bodily functions, greater ratios of <a href="/wiki/Brain-to-body_mass_ratio" class="mw-redirect" title="Brain-to-body mass ratio">brain to body mass</a> may increase the amount of brain mass available for more complex cognitive tasks. <a href="/wiki/Allometric" class="mw-redirect" title="Allometric">Allometric</a> analysis indicates that mammalian brain size scales at approximately the <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1154941027">.mw-parser-output .frac{white-space:nowrap}.mw-parser-output .frac .num,.mw-parser-output .frac .den{font-size:80%;line-height:0;vertical-align:super}.mw-parser-output .frac .den{vertical-align:sub}.mw-parser-output .sr-only{border:0;clip:rect(0,0,0,0);clip-path:polygon(0px 0px,0px 0px,0px 0px);height:1px;margin:-1px;overflow:hidden;padding:0;position:absolute;width:1px}</style><span class="frac"><span class="num">2</span>&#8260;<span class="den">3</span></span> or <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1154941027" /><span class="frac"><span class="num">3</span>&#8260;<span class="den">4</span></span> exponent of the body mass. Comparison of a particular animal's brain size with the expected brain size based on such allometric analysis provides an <a href="/wiki/Encephalization_quotient" title="Encephalization quotient">encephalisation quotient</a> that can be used as another indication of animal intelligence.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-218" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Sperm_whale" title="Sperm whale">Sperm whales</a> have the largest brain mass of any animal on earth, averaging 8,000 cubic centimetres (490&#160;cu&#160;in) and 7.8 kilograms (17&#160;lb) in mature males.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-219" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Self-awareness" title="Self-awareness">Self-awareness</a> appears to be a sign of abstract thinking. Self-awareness, although not well-defined, is believed to be a precursor to more advanced processes such as <a href="/wiki/Metacognition" title="Metacognition">metacognitive reasoning</a>. The traditional method for measuring this is the <a href="/wiki/Mirror_test" title="Mirror test">mirror test</a>, which determines if an animal possesses the ability of self-recognition.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-220" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals that have passed the mirror test include <a href="/wiki/Asian_elephant" title="Asian elephant">Asian elephants</a> (some pass, some do not);<sup id="cite&#95;ref-221" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> chimpanzees;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-robert&#95;222-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-robert-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Bonobo" title="Bonobo">bonobos</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-223" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Orangutan" title="Orangutan">orangutans</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-orangutan&#95;224-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-orangutan-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> humans, from 18 months (<a href="/wiki/Mirror_stage" title="Mirror stage">mirror stage</a>);<sup id="cite&#95;ref-archer&#95;225-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-archer-225"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Common_bottlenose_dolphin" title="Common bottlenose dolphin">common bottlenose dolphins</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-227" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-227"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>a<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-parker95&#95;226-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-parker95-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Orca" title="Orca">orcas</a>;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Delfour&#95;228-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Delfour-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/False_killer_whale" title="False killer whale">false killer whales</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Delfour&#95;228-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Delfour-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Social_structure">Social structure</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Social_animal" class="mw-redirect" title="Social animal">Social animal</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Borneo_elephants.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Borneo_elephants.png/250px-Borneo_elephants.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Borneo_elephants.png/500px-Borneo_elephants.png 2x" data-file-width="650" data-file-height="650" /></a><figcaption>Female elephants live in stable groups, along with their offspring</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Eusociality" title="Eusociality">Eusociality</a> is the highest level of social organisation. These societies have an overlap of adult generations, the division of reproductive labour and cooperative caring of young. Usually insects, such as <a href="/wiki/Bee" title="Bee">bees</a>, ants and termites, have eusocial behaviour, but it is demonstrated in two rodent species: the naked mole-rat<sup id="cite&#95;ref-229" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Damaraland_mole-rat" title="Damaraland mole-rat">Damaraland mole-rat</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-230" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Presociality is when animals exhibit more than just sexual interactions with members of the same species, but fall short of qualifying as eusocial. That is, presocial animals can display communal living, cooperative care of young, or primitive division of reproductive labour, but they do not display all of the three essential traits of eusocial animals. Humans and some species of <a href="/wiki/Callitrichidae" title="Callitrichidae">Callitrichidae</a> (<a href="/wiki/Marmoset" title="Marmoset">marmosets</a> and <a href="/wiki/Tamarin" title="Tamarin">tamarins</a>) are unique among primates in their degree of cooperative care of young.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-231" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Harry_Harlow" title="Harry Harlow">Harry Harlow</a> set up an experiment with <a href="/wiki/Rhesus_monkey" class="mw-redirect" title="Rhesus monkey">rhesus monkeys</a>, presocial primates, in 1958; the results from this study showed that social encounters are necessary in order for the young monkeys to develop both mentally and sexually.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Harlow71&#95;232-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Harlow71-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A <a href="/wiki/Fission%E2%80%93fusion_society" title="Fission–fusion society">fission–fusion society</a> is a society that changes frequently in its size and composition, making up a permanent social group called the "parent group". Permanent social networks consist of all individual members of a community and often varies to track changes in their environment. In a fission–fusion society, the main parent group can fracture (fission) into smaller stable subgroups or individuals to adapt to <a href="/wiki/Social_environment" title="Social environment">environmental</a> or social circumstances. For example, a number of males may break off from the main group in order to hunt or forage for food during the day, but at night they may return to join (fusion) the primary group to share food and partake in other activities. Many mammals exhibit this, such as primates (for example orangutans and <a href="/wiki/Spider_monkey" title="Spider monkey">spider monkeys</a>),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-233" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> elephants,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-234" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Spotted_hyena" title="Spotted hyena">spotted hyenas</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-235" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> lions,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-236" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and dolphins.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Solitary animals defend a territory and avoid social interactions with the members of its species, except during breeding season. This is to avoid resource competition, as two individuals of the same species would occupy the same niche, and to prevent depletion of food.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-238" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A solitary animal, while foraging, can also be less conspicuous to predators or prey.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-239" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Fighting_red_kangaroos_2.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Fighting_red_kangaroos_2.jpg/250px-Fighting_red_kangaroos_2.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="190" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Fighting_red_kangaroos_2.jpg/500px-Fighting_red_kangaroos_2.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2250" data-file-height="1710" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Red_kangaroo" title="Red kangaroo">Red kangaroos</a> "boxing" for <a href="/wiki/Dominance_hierarchy" title="Dominance hierarchy">dominance</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>In a <a href="/wiki/Dominance_hierarchy" title="Dominance hierarchy">hierarchy</a>, individuals are either dominant or submissive. A despotic hierarchy is where one individual is dominant while the others are submissive, as in wolves and lemurs,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-240" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and a <a href="/wiki/Pecking_order" class="mw-redirect" title="Pecking order">pecking order</a> is a linear ranking of individuals where there is a top individual and a bottom individual. Pecking orders may also be ranked by sex, where the lowest individual of a sex has a higher ranking than the top individual of the other sex, as in hyenas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-241" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Dominant individuals, or alphas, have a high chance of reproductive success, especially in <a href="/wiki/Harem_(zoology)" title="Harem (zoology)">harems</a> where one or a few males (resident males) have exclusive breeding rights to females in a group.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-242" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Non-resident males can also be accepted in harems, but some species, such as the <a href="/wiki/Common_vampire_bat" title="Common vampire bat">common vampire bat</a> (<i>Desmodus rotundus</i>), may be more strict.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-243" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Some mammals are perfectly <a href="/wiki/Monogamy_in_animals" title="Monogamy in animals">monogamous</a>, meaning that they <a href="/wiki/Pair_bond" title="Pair bond">mate for life</a> and take no other partners (even after the original mate's death), as with wolves, <a href="/wiki/Eurasian_beaver" title="Eurasian beaver">Eurasian beavers</a>, and otters.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-244" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-245" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There are three types of polygamy: either one or multiple dominant males have breeding rights (<a href="/wiki/Polygyny_in_animals" title="Polygyny in animals">polygyny</a>), multiple males that females mate with (polyandry), or multiple males have exclusive relations with multiple females (<a href="/wiki/Polygynandry" title="Polygynandry">polygynandry</a>). It is much more common for polygynous mating to happen, which, excluding <a href="/wiki/Lek_mating" title="Lek mating">leks</a>, are estimated to occur in up to 90% of mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-246" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Lek mating occurs when males congregate around females and try to attract them with various <a href="/wiki/Courtship_display" title="Courtship display">courtship displays</a> and vocalisations, as in harbour seals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-247" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>All <a href="/wiki/Higher_mammal" class="mw-redirect" title="Higher mammal">higher mammals</a> (excluding monotremes) share two major adaptations for care of the young: live birth and lactation. These imply a group-wide choice of a degree of <a href="/wiki/Parental_care" title="Parental care">parental care</a>. They may build nests and dig burrows to raise their young in, or feed and guard them often for a prolonged period of time. Many mammals are <a href="/wiki/K-selected" class="mw-redirect" title="K-selected">K-selected</a>, and invest more time and energy into their young than do <a href="/wiki/R-selected" class="mw-redirect" title="R-selected">r-selected</a> animals. When two animals mate, they both share an interest in the success of the offspring, though often to different extremes. Mammalian females exhibit some degree of maternal aggression, another example of parental care, which may be targeted against other females of the species or the young of other females; however, some mammals may "aunt" the infants of other females, and care for them. Mammalian males may play a role in child rearing, as with <a href="/wiki/Tenrec" title="Tenrec">tenrecs</a>, however this varies species to species, even within the same genus. For example, the males of the <a href="/wiki/Southern_pig-tailed_macaque" title="Southern pig-tailed macaque">southern pig-tailed macaque</a> (<i>Macaca nemestrina</i>) do not participate in child care, whereas the males of the <a href="/wiki/Japanese_macaque" title="Japanese macaque">Japanese macaque</a> (<i>M. fuscata</i>) do.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-248" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Humans_and_other_mammals">Humans and other mammals</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Human_uses_of_mammals" title="Human uses of mammals">Human uses of mammals</a></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="In_human_culture">In human culture</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Lascaux_painting.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Lascaux_painting.jpg/250px-Lascaux_painting.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="164" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Lascaux_painting.jpg/500px-Lascaux_painting.jpg 2x" data-file-width="720" data-file-height="472" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic" title="Upper Paleolithic">Upper Paleolithic</a> <a href="/wiki/Cave_painting" title="Cave painting">cave painting</a> of a variety of large mammals, <a href="/wiki/Lascaux" title="Lascaux">Lascaux</a>, <abbr title="circa">c.</abbr><span style="white-space:nowrap;">&#8201;17,300</span> years old</figcaption></figure>
<p>Non-human mammals play a wide variety of roles in human culture. They are the most popular of <a href="/wiki/Pet" title="Pet">pets</a>, with tens of millions of dogs, cats and other animals including <a href="/wiki/Rabbit" title="Rabbit">rabbits</a> and mice kept by families around the world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-249" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-250" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-251" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals such as <a href="/wiki/Mammoth" title="Mammoth">mammoths</a>, horses and deer are among the earliest subjects of art, being found in <a href="/wiki/Upper_Paleolithic" title="Upper Paleolithic">Upper Paleolithic</a> <a href="/wiki/Cave_painting" title="Cave painting">cave paintings</a> such as at <a href="/wiki/Lascaux" title="Lascaux">Lascaux</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-252" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Major artists such as <a href="/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer" title="Albrecht Dürer">Albrecht Dürer</a>, <a href="/wiki/George_Stubbs" title="George Stubbs">George Stubbs</a> and <a href="/wiki/Edwin_Landseer" title="Edwin Landseer">Edwin Landseer</a> are known for their portraits of mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Jones&#95;253-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Jones-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Many species of mammals have been <a href="/wiki/Hunting" title="Hunting">hunted</a> for sport and for food; deer and <a href="/wiki/Wild_boar" title="Wild boar">wild boar</a> are especially popular as <a href="/wiki/Game_(hunting)" title="Game (hunting)">game animals</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-254" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-255" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-256" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals such as <a href="/wiki/Horse_racing" title="Horse racing">horses</a> and <a href="/wiki/Greyhound_racing" title="Greyhound racing">dogs</a> are widely raced for sport, often combined with <a href="/wiki/Gambling" title="Gambling">betting on the outcome</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-257" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-258" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> There is a tension between the role of animals as companions to humans, and their existence as individuals with <a href="/wiki/Animal_rights" title="Animal rights">rights of their own</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-259" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammals further play a wide variety of roles in literature,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-260" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-261" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-262" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> film,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-263" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> mythology, and religion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-264" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-265" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-266" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Uses_and_importance">Uses and importance</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Livestock" title="Livestock">Livestock</a>, <a href="/wiki/Laboratory_animal" class="mw-redirect" title="Laboratory animal">Laboratory animal</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Pack_animal" title="Pack animal">Pack animal</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Hand_milking_a_cow_at_Cobbes_Farm_Museum.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Hand_milking_a_cow_at_Cobbes_Farm_Museum.jpg/250px-Hand_milking_a_cow_at_Cobbes_Farm_Museum.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="253" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Hand_milking_a_cow_at_Cobbes_Farm_Museum.jpg/500px-Hand_milking_a_cow_at_Cobbes_Farm_Museum.jpg 2x" data-file-width="900" data-file-height="1200" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Cattle" title="Cattle">Cattle</a> have been <a href="/wiki/Dairy_farming" title="Dairy farming">kept for milk</a> for thousands of years.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The domestication of mammals was instrumental in the <a href="/wiki/Neolithic_Revolution" title="Neolithic Revolution">Neolithic development of agriculture</a> and of <a href="/wiki/Civilisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Civilisation">civilisation</a>, causing farmers to replace <a href="/wiki/Hunter-gatherer" title="Hunter-gatherer">hunter-gatherers</a> around the world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>b<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Larson&#95;269-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Larson-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This transition from hunting and gathering to <a href="/wiki/Pastoralism" title="Pastoralism">herding flocks</a> and <a href="/wiki/Agriculture" title="Agriculture">growing crops</a> was a major step in human history. The new agricultural economies, based on domesticated mammals, caused "radical restructuring of human societies, worldwide alterations in biodiversity, and significant changes in the Earth's landforms and its atmosphere... momentous outcomes".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-270" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Domestication" title="Domestication">Domestic</a> mammals form a large part of the <a href="/wiki/Livestock" title="Livestock">livestock</a> raised for <a href="/wiki/Meat" title="Meat">meat</a> across the world. They include (2009) around 1.4&#160;billion <a href="/wiki/Cattle" title="Cattle">cattle</a>, 1&#160;billion <a href="/wiki/Sheep" title="Sheep">sheep</a>, 1&#160;billion <a href="/wiki/Domestic_pig" class="mw-redirect" title="Domestic pig">domestic pigs</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-272" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and (1985) over 700&#160;million rabbits.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-273" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Working_animal" title="Working animal">Working domestic animals</a> including cattle and horses have been used for work and <a href="/wiki/Transport" title="Transport">transport</a> from the origins of agriculture, their numbers declining with the arrival of mechanised transport and <a href="/wiki/Agricultural_machinery" title="Agricultural machinery">agricultural machinery</a>. In 2004 they still provided some 80% of the power for the mainly small farms in the third world, and some 20% of the world's transport, again mainly in rural areas. In mountainous regions unsuitable for wheeled vehicles, <a href="/wiki/Pack_animal" title="Pack animal">pack animals</a> continue to transport goods.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Pond2004&#95;274-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Pond2004-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Mammal skins provide <a href="/wiki/Leather" title="Leather">leather</a> for <a href="/wiki/Shoe" title="Shoe">shoes</a>, <a href="/wiki/Clothing" title="Clothing">clothing</a> and <a href="/wiki/Upholstery" title="Upholstery">upholstery</a>. <a href="/wiki/Wool" title="Wool">Wool</a> from mammals including sheep, goats and <a href="/wiki/Alpaca" title="Alpaca">alpacas</a> has been used for centuries for clothing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-275" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-276" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Distribution_of_Mammals_on_Earth.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Distribution_of_Mammals_on_Earth.png/500px-Distribution_of_Mammals_on_Earth.png" decoding="async" width="400" height="467" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Distribution_of_Mammals_on_Earth.png/960px-Distribution_of_Mammals_on_Earth.png 2x" data-file-width="6450" data-file-height="7533" /></a><figcaption>Livestock make up 62% of the world's mammal biomass; humans account for 34%; and wild mammals are just 4%<sup id="cite&#95;ref-277" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></figcaption></figure>
<p>Mammals serve a major role in science as <a href="/wiki/Animal_model" class="mw-redirect" title="Animal model">experimental animals</a>, both in fundamental biological research, such as in genetics,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-278" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and in the development of new medicines, which must be tested exhaustively to demonstrate their <a href="/wiki/Pharmacovigilance" title="Pharmacovigilance">safety</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-279" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Millions of mammals, especially mice and rats, are used in <a href="/wiki/Animal_testing" title="Animal testing">experiments</a> each year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EUstatistics2013&#95;280-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EUstatistics2013-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Knockout_mouse" title="Knockout mouse">knockout mouse</a> is a <a href="/wiki/Genetically_modified_mouse" title="Genetically modified mouse">genetically modified mouse</a> with an inactivated <a href="/wiki/Gene" title="Gene">gene</a>, replaced or disrupted with an artificial piece of DNA. They enable the study of <a href="/wiki/Sequencing" title="Sequencing">sequenced</a> genes whose functions are unknown.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-281" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A small percentage of the mammals are non-human primates, used in research for their similarity to humans.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-282" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Carlsson2004&#95;283-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Carlsson2004-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>281<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Weatherall&#95;etal2006&#95;284-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Weatherall_etal2006-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Despite the benefits domesticated mammals had for human development, humans have an increasingly detrimental effect on wild mammals across the world. It has been estimated that the mass of all <i>wild</i> mammals has declined to only 4% of all mammals, with 96% of mammals being humans and their livestock now (see figure). In fact, terrestrial wild mammals make up only 2% of all mammals.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-285" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bar-On&#95;2018&#95;286-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bar-On_2018-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Hybrids">Hybrids</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Hybrid_(biology)" title="Hybrid (biology)">Hybrid (biology)</a></div>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1275594942">@media all and (max-width:720px){.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery{width:100%!important}}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery{display:table}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-default{background:transparent;margin-top:4px}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-center{margin-left:auto;margin-right:auto}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-left{float:left}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-right{float:right}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-none{float:none}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-center .gallery{justify-content:center}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-left .gallery{justify-content:left}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-right .gallery{justify-content:right}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery-collapsible{width:100%}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .title,.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .main,.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .footer{display:table-row}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .title>div{display:table-cell;padding:0 4px 4px;text-align:center;font-weight:bold}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .main>div{display:table-cell}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .gallery.gallery.gallery{line-height:1.35em;display:flex;flex-wrap:wrap;column-gap:4px}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .footer>div{display:table-cell;padding:4px;text-align:right;font-size:85%;line-height:1em}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .title>div *,.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .footer>div *{overflow:visible}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .gallerybox img{background:none!important}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .bordered-images .thumb img{outline:solid var(--background-color-neutral,#eaecf0)1px}.mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .whitebg .thumb{background:var(--background-color-base,#fff)!important}@media screen{html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .bordered-images .thumb img[alt*="\200b \200b \200b "],html.skin-theme-clientpref-night .mw-parser-output .skin-invert-image .mod-gallery .whitebg .thumb.thumb.thumb
