(prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .bordered-images .thumb img[alt*="\200b \200b \200b "],html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .skin-invert-image .mod-gallery .whitebg .thumb.thumb.thumb img{outline:solid #d7d7d7 1px}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .skin-invert-image .mod-gallery .whitebg .thumb.thumb.thumb img{background:none!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .mod-gallery .whitebg .thumb img:not([alt*="\200b \200b \200b "]):not([alt*="\200b \200b \200c "]){background:white!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .mod-gallery img[alt*="\200b \200b \200b "]{filter:invert(1)hue-rotate(180deg)}}</style><div class="mod-gallery mod-gallery-default mod-gallery-left"><div class="main"><div><ul class="nochecker bordered-images whitebg gallery mw-gallery-traditional">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 235px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 230px; height: 170px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Equus_quagga_quagga,_coloured.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Equus_quagga_quagga%2C_coloured.jpg/250px-Equus_quagga_quagga%2C_coloured.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="140" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Equus_quagga_quagga%2C_coloured.jpg/500px-Equus_quagga_quagga%2C_coloured.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1138" data-file-height="798" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"></div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 235px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 230px; height: 170px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Rau_Quagga_on_Devils_Peak.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Rau_Quagga_on_Devils_Peak.jpg/250px-Rau_Quagga_on_Devils_Peak.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="129" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Rau_Quagga_on_Devils_Peak.jpg/500px-Rau_Quagga_on_Devils_Peak.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1400" data-file-height="904" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext"></div>
		</li>
</ul></div></div><div class="footer"><div><div style="float: left;">A true <a href="/wiki/Quagga" title="Quagga">quagga</a>, 1870 (left) vs. a <a href="/wiki/Quagga_Project" title="Quagga Project">bred-back quagga</a>, 2014 (right)</div></div></div></div>
<p>Hybrids are offspring resulting from the breeding of two genetically distinct individuals, which usually will result in a high degree of heterozygosity, though hybrid and heterozygous are not synonymous. The deliberate or accidental hybridising of two or more species of closely related animals through captive breeding is a human activity which has been in existence for millennia and has grown for economic purposes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-287" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Hybrids between different subspecies within a species (such as between the <a href="/wiki/Bengal_tiger" title="Bengal tiger">Bengal tiger</a> and <a href="/wiki/Siberian_tiger" title="Siberian tiger">Siberian tiger</a>) are known as intra-specific hybrids. Hybrids between different species within the same genus (such as between lions and tigers) are known as interspecific hybrids or crosses. Hybrids between different genera (such as between sheep and goats) are known as intergeneric hybrids.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-288" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>286<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Natural hybrids will occur in <a href="/wiki/Hybrid_zone" title="Hybrid zone">hybrid zones</a>, where two populations of species within the same genera or species living in the same or adjacent areas will interbreed with each other. Some hybrids have been recognised as species, such as the <a href="/wiki/Red_wolf" title="Red wolf">red wolf</a> (though this is controversial).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-289" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Artificial_selection" class="mw-redirect" title="Artificial selection">Artificial selection</a>, the deliberate <a href="/wiki/Selective_breeding" title="Selective breeding">selective breeding</a> of domestic animals, is being used to <a href="/wiki/Breeding_back" title="Breeding back">breed back</a> <a href="/wiki/Holocene_extinction" title="Holocene extinction">recently extinct</a> animals in an attempt to achieve an animal breed with a <a href="/wiki/Phenotype" title="Phenotype">phenotype</a> that resembles that extinct <a href="/wiki/Wildtype" class="mw-redirect" title="Wildtype">wildtype</a> ancestor. A breeding-back (intraspecific) hybrid may be very similar to the extinct wildtype in appearance, ecological niche and to some extent genetics, but the initial <a href="/wiki/Gene_pool" title="Gene pool">gene pool</a> of that wild type is lost forever with its <a href="/wiki/Extinction" title="Extinction">extinction</a>. As a result, bred-back breeds are at best vague look-alikes of extinct wildtypes, as <a href="/wiki/Heck_cattle" title="Heck cattle">Heck cattle</a> are of the <a href="/wiki/Aurochs" title="Aurochs">aurochs</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-vanVuure&#95;290-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-vanVuure-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Purebred" title="Purebred">Purebred</a> wild species evolved to a specific ecology can be threatened with extinction<sup id="cite&#95;ref-291" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> through the process of <a href="/wiki/Genetic_pollution" title="Genetic pollution">genetic pollution</a>, the uncontrolled hybridisation, <a href="/wiki/Introgression" title="Introgression">introgression</a> genetic swamping which leads to homogenisation or <a href="/wiki/Fitness_(biology)" title="Fitness (biology)">out-competition</a> from the <a href="/wiki/Heterosis" title="Heterosis">heterosic</a> hybrid species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-292" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> When new populations are imported or selectively bred by people, or when habitat modification brings previously isolated species into contact, extinction in some species, especially rare varieties, is possible.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-293" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Interbreeding" class="mw-redirect" title="Interbreeding">Interbreeding</a> can swamp the rarer gene pool and create hybrids, depleting the purebred gene pool. For example, the endangered <a href="/wiki/Wild_water_buffalo" title="Wild water buffalo">wild water buffalo</a> is most threatened with extinction by genetic pollution from the <a href="/wiki/Water_buffalo" title="Water buffalo">domestic water buffalo</a>. Such extinctions are not always apparent from a <a href="/wiki/Morphology_(biology)" title="Morphology (biology)">morphological</a> standpoint. Some degree of <a href="/wiki/Gene_flow" title="Gene flow">gene flow</a> is a normal evolutionary process, nevertheless, hybridisation threatens the existence of rare species.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-294" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-295" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Threats">Threats</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Holocene_extinction" title="Holocene extinction">Holocene extinction</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Extinctions_Africa_Austrailia_NAmerica_Madagascar.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ad/Extinctions_Africa_Austrailia_NAmerica_Madagascar.gif" decoding="async" width="350" height="359" class="mw-file-element" data-file-width="441" data-file-height="452" /></a><figcaption>Biodiversity of large mammal species per continent before and after humans arrived there</figcaption></figure>
<p>The loss of species from ecological communities, <a href="/wiki/Defaunation" title="Defaunation">defaunation</a>, is primarily driven by human activity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-dirzo&#95;296-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dirzo-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This has resulted in <a href="/wiki/Empty_forest" title="Empty forest">empty forests</a>, ecological communities depleted of large vertebrates.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-primack2014&#95;297-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-primack2014-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-298" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/Quaternary_extinction_event" class="mw-redirect" title="Quaternary extinction event">Quaternary extinction event</a>, the mass die-off of <a href="/wiki/Megafauna" title="Megafauna">megafaunal</a> variety coincided with the appearance of humans, suggesting a human influence. One hypothesis is that humans hunted large mammals, such as the <a href="/wiki/Woolly_mammoth" title="Woolly mammoth">woolly mammoth</a>, into extinction.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-299" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-300" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The 2019 <i><a href="/wiki/Global_Assessment_Report_on_Biodiversity_and_Ecosystem_Services" title="Global Assessment Report on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services">Global Assessment Report on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services</a></i> by <a href="/wiki/IPBES" class="mw-redirect" title="IPBES">IPBES</a> states that the total <a href="/wiki/Biomass_(ecology)" title="Biomass (ecology)">biomass</a> of wild mammals has declined by 82 per cent since the beginning of human civilisation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-301" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-302" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Wild animals make up just 4% of mammalian biomass on earth, while humans and their domesticated animals make up 96%.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Bar-On&#95;2018&#95;286-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Bar-On_2018-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Various species are predicted to <a href="/wiki/List_of_critically_endangered_species" class="mw-redirect" title="List of critically endangered species">become extinct in the near future</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-303" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-303"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> among them the <a href="/wiki/Rhinoceros" title="Rhinoceros">rhinoceros</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-304" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Giraffe" title="Giraffe">giraffes</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-305" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and species of <a href="/wiki/Primate" title="Primate">primates</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-primates&#95;306-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-primates-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Pangolin" title="Pangolin">pangolins</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-307" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-307"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>305<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to the WWF's 2020 <i><a href="/wiki/Living_Planet_Report" title="Living Planet Report">Living Planet Report</a></i>, vertebrate <a href="/wiki/Wildlife" title="Wildlife">wildlife</a> populations have declined by 68% since 1970 as a result of human activities, particularly <a href="/wiki/Overconsumption" title="Overconsumption">overconsumption</a>, <a href="/wiki/Population_growth" title="Population growth">population growth</a> and <a href="/wiki/Intensive_farming" title="Intensive farming">intensive farming</a>, which is evidence that humans have triggered a <a href="/wiki/Sixth_mass_extinction" class="mw-redirect" title="Sixth mass extinction">sixth mass extinction</a> event.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-308" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-308"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-309" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-309"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>307<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Hunting alone threatens hundreds of mammalian species around the world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-310" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>308<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-311" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>309<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Scientists claim that the growing demand for <a href="/wiki/Meat" title="Meat">meat</a> is contributing to <a href="/wiki/Biodiversity_loss" title="Biodiversity loss">biodiversity loss</a> as this is a significant driver of <a href="/wiki/Deforestation" title="Deforestation">deforestation</a> and <a href="/wiki/Habitat_destruction" title="Habitat destruction">habitat destruction</a>; species-rich habitats, such as significant portions of the <a href="/wiki/Amazon_rainforest" title="Amazon rainforest">Amazon rainforest</a>, are being converted to agricultural land for meat production.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-312" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-312"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>310<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-313" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-313"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>311<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-314" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-314"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>312<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Another influence is over-hunting and <a href="/wiki/Species_affected_by_poaching" title="Species affected by poaching">poaching</a>, which can reduce the overall population of game animals,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-315" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-315"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>313<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> especially those located near villages,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-peres2006&#95;316-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-peres2006-316"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> as in the case of <a href="/wiki/Peccary" title="Peccary">peccaries</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-317" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-317"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>315<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The effects of poaching can especially be seen in the <a href="/wiki/Ivory_trade" title="Ivory trade">ivory trade</a> with African elephants.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-318" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-318"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Marine mammals are at risk from entanglement from fishing gear, notably <a href="/wiki/Cetacean_bycatch" title="Cetacean bycatch">cetaceans</a>, with discard mortalities ranging from 65,000 to 86,000 individuals annually.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-319" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-319"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Attention is being given to endangered species globally, notably through the <a href="/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity" title="Convention on Biological Diversity">Convention on Biological Diversity</a>, otherwise known as the Rio Accord, which includes 189 signatory countries that are focused on identifying endangered species and habitats.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-320" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-320"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>318<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Another notable conservation organisation is the IUCN, which has a membership of over 1,200 governmental and <a href="/wiki/Non-governmental_organization" title="Non-governmental organization">non-governmental</a> organisations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-321" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-321"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>319<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/List_of_recently_extinct_mammals" title="List of recently extinct mammals">Recent extinctions</a> can be directly attributed to human influences.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ceballos&#95;322-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ceballos-322"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>320<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-dirzo&#95;296-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-dirzo-296"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The IUCN characterises 'recent' extinction as those that have occurred past the cut-off point of 1500,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-323" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-323"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>321<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and around 80 mammal species have gone extinct since that time and 2015.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-324" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>322<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some species, such as the <a href="/wiki/P%C3%A8re_David%27s_deer" title="Père David&#39;s deer">Père David's deer</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-325" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-325"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>323<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> are <a href="/wiki/Extinct_in_the_wild" title="Extinct in the wild">extinct in the wild</a>, and survive solely in captive populations. Other species, such as the <a href="/wiki/Florida_panther" title="Florida panther">Florida panther</a>, are <a href="/wiki/Ecological_extinction" title="Ecological extinction">ecologically extinct</a>, surviving in such low numbers that they essentially have no impact on the ecosystem.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mckinney2013&#95;326-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mckinney2013-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>324<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 318">&#58;&#8202;318&#8202;</span></sup> Other populations are only <a href="/wiki/Local_extinction" title="Local extinction">locally extinct</a> (extirpated), still existing elsewhere, but reduced in distribution,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-mckinney2013&#95;326-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mckinney2013-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>324<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 75–77">&#58;&#8202;75–77&#8202;</span></sup> as with the extinction of <a href="/wiki/Grey_whale" class="mw-redirect" title="Grey whale">grey whales</a> in the <a href="/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean" title="Atlantic Ocean">Atlantic</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-327" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-327"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>325<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<li id="cite&#95;note-227"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-227">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Decreased latency to approach the mirror, repetitious head circling and close viewing of the marked areas were considered signs of self-recognition since they do not have arms and cannot touch the marked areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-parker95&#95;226-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-parker95-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-268"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-268">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Diamond discussed this matter further in his 1997 book <i><a href="/wiki/Guns,_Germs,_and_Steel" title="Guns, Germs, and Steel">Guns, Germs, and Steel</a></i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-267" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>

</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-7"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-7">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.iucnredlist.org/initiatives/mammals">"Mammals"</a>. <i>The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species</i>. <a href="/wiki/International_Union_for_Conservation_of_Nature" title="International Union for Conservation of Nature">International Union for Conservation of Nature</a> (IUCN). April 2010. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20160903200637/http://www.iucnredlist.org/initiatives/mammals">Archived</a> from the original on 3 September 2016<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">23 August</span> 2016</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=The+IUCN+Red+List+of+Threatened+Species&amp;rft.atitle=Mammals&amp;rft.date=2010-04&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.iucnredlist.org%2Finitiatives%2Fmammals&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AMammal" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
